proposals.title: Alternative Sourcing of Bioactive Lignans: Investigation of Leaf and Corm Tissues for the Sustainable Production of Podophyllotoxin-Related Compounds
proposals.motivation: Podophyllotoxin and its related lignans are crucial precursors for chemotherapeutic agents like etoposide, but their primary botanical source, the Himalayan mayapple (Podophyllum hexandrum), is endangered due to destructive harvesting of its tuber-barks. This creates a supply bottleneck and conservation crisis. Identifying alternative, renewable plant parts from known or related species for these bioactive compounds is a pressing need in natural product chemistry to ensure sustainable and scalable production.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that key anti-cancer lignans, such as podophyllotoxin or its analogs, are biosynthesized and accumulated not only in the tuber-barks but also in the more accessible and renewable aerial parts (leaves) or storage organs (corms) of Podophyllum species or phylogenetically related plants.
proposals.method: We will select Podophyllum hexandrum and related species (e.g., P. peltatum) for study. Leaves, corms, and traditional tuber-barks (as control) will be extracted sequentially with methanol and fractionated. Bioactive lignans will be targeted using LC-MS/MS analysis with multiple reaction monitoring (MRM) based on known fragmentation patterns of podophyllotoxin and its derivatives. Isolated compounds from positive tissues will be structurally elucidated via NMR (1H, 13C, 2D) and HR-ESI-MS. Their anti-proliferative activity will be validated against human cancer cell lines (e.g., A549, MCF-7) using MTT assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, sustainable sourcing strategy for valuable lignans, reducing pressure on endangered root stocks. It will expand the phytochemical profile of Podophyllum species by documenting lignan distribution across tissues. The findings could directly inform cultivation and harvesting protocols for improved yield and conservation.
proposals.novelty: While prior research focuses on tuber-barks as the exclusive lignan source, this proposal systematically challenges that paradigm by targeting understudied, renewable plant parts within the same species, a strategy not commonly applied to podophyllotoxin sourcing.

proposals.title: Identification and Evaluation of Melanogenesis-Stimulating Compounds from Natural Sources for Potential Vitiligo Therapy
proposals.motivation: Vitiligo is a chronic depigmentation disorder with limited and often unsatisfactory treatment options, highlighting a significant unmet medical need. The search for safe and effective agents to stimulate melanocyte function and melanin production is a critical area of dermatological research. Natural products have historically been a rich source of bioactive compounds with modulatory effects on pigmentation pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that certain natural compounds, previously isolated for their diverse bioactivities, can function as potent agonists of the melanogenic pathway by upregulating key enzymes like tyrosinase and MITF, thereby promoting melanogenesis in vitro and ex vivo.
proposals.method: We will screen a library of pre-isolated compounds from Dr. Kim's previous work (e.g., prenylhydroquinone glycosides, methoxyflavones, and other phenolic acids) for melanogenesis-promoting activity in B16F10 mouse melanoma cells and normal human melanocytes (HEMn-MPs). Primary screening will measure intracellular tyrosinase activity and melanin content. Hit compounds will be further evaluated for their effects on the expression of MITF, tyrosinase, TRP-1, and TRP-2 via western blot and qRT-PCR. The most promising candidate will be tested in a murine melanocyte-keratinocyte co-culture model and in ex vivo human skin explants to assess physiological relevance.
proposals.contribution: This work will identify novel natural product-derived leads for vitiligo repigmentation therapy, expanding the pharmacological toolkit beyond conventional treatments like corticosteroids and UV phototherapy. It will provide mechanistic insights into how specific phytochemical scaffolds activate the melanogenic cascade, contributing to the fundamental understanding of pigmentation biology.
proposals.novelty: This proposal innovatively repurposes a library of compounds, originally isolated for anti-melanogenic or other activities, to pursue the opposite pharmacological goal of stimulating melanogenesis, a strategy underexplored in natural product research prior to 2026.

proposals.title: In Vivo Evaluation of Novel Monoglyceride and Fatty Acid Derivatives from Natural Sources for the Treatment of Hyperpigmentation Disorders
proposals.motivation: Hyperpigmentation disorders, such as melasma and post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation, are significant dermatological concerns with limited treatment options that often have side effects like irritation. Natural product-derived compounds offer a promising avenue for safer, targeted therapies. Building on our recent isolation of new monoglyceride and fatty acid derivatives with in vitro anti-melanogenic activity, their efficacy must be validated in a physiologically relevant in vivo system to assess therapeutic potential.
proposals.hypothesis: The newly isolated monoglyceride and fatty acid derivatives will significantly reduce hyperpigmentation in an animal model by inhibiting melanogenesis through the modulation of key pathways (e.g., MITF, tyrosinase) without inducing skin toxicity.
proposals.method: We will employ a UVB-induced hyperpigmentation model in C57BL/6 mice. The isolated compounds will be formulated into a topical cream and applied daily to the dorsal skin of mice following UVB irradiation. The antimelanogenic efficacy will be quantified by measuring skin lightness (L* value) using a chromameter, analyzing melanin content in skin sections via Fontana-Masson staining, and assessing tyrosinase activity. Histological evaluation (H&E staining) will be performed to monitor skin irritation. The positive control will be a commercial agent like kojic acid, and a vehicle cream will serve as the negative control.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first in vivo evidence for the anti-melanogenic efficacy of these specific natural compound classes, moving them from in vitro hits toward potential lead compounds for cosmetic or pharmaceutical development. It will establish a critical proof-of-concept for targeting hyperpigmentation with novel lipid-based natural products and elucidate their mechanism of action in a living system.
proposals.novelty: While many plant-derived phenolics have been tested in vivo for skin lightening, systematic in vivo evaluation of monoglyceride and fatty acid derivatives from natural sources for this purpose is highly underexplored. This work directly translates our unique chemical discoveries into a validated biological context.

proposals.title: Untargeted Metabolomic Profiling and Bioactivity-Guided Isolation of Novel Anti-Neuroinflammatory Compounds from Colocasia antiquorum Tuber-Barks
proposals.motivation: Colocasia antiquorum (eddoe) is a traditional food and medicinal plant, but its tuber-bark, a significant agricultural byproduct, remains largely unexplored for its phytochemical composition and bioactivity. Given the rising interest in natural products for complex diseases like neuroinflammation, a comprehensive chemical inventory of this underutilized resource is needed. Pre-2026, untargeted metabolomics has proven powerful for dereplicating known compounds and highlighting novel chemical entities in plant matrices, offering a strategic starting point for drug discovery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an untargeted metabolomic approach will reveal a diverse array of unreported secondary metabolites in C. antiquorum tuber-barks, and that bioactivity-guided fractionation of this complex extract will lead to the isolation of novel compounds with significant anti-neuroinflammatory activity.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-step LC-MS/MS-based untargeted metabolomics workflow. First, crude extracts will be analyzed using high-resolution Q-TOF mass spectrometry coupled with reversed-phase UHPLC. Data will be processed using MZmine 2.53 for feature detection, alignment, and annotation against pre-2026 spectral libraries (GNPS, MassBank). Putative novel features will be prioritized using molecular networking on the GNPS platform. Second, bioactive fractions showing inhibition of nitric oxide production in LPS-stimulated BV-2 microglial cells will undergo repeated chromatographic separation (including HPLC and prep-TLC) for compound isolation, with structures elucidated via NMR and HRMS.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive secondary metabolite map of C. antiquorum tuber-barks, contributing to the chemotaxonomy of the Araceae family. It is expected to yield several new chemical entities, expanding the library of natural product scaffolds. The discovery of bioactive compounds will validate the traditional use of the plant and provide new lead molecules for mitigating neuroinflammation.
proposals.novelty: While metabolomics is established, its application specifically to the understudied tuber-bark waste of C. antiquorum for anti-neuroinflammatory drug discovery is novel. The integrated approach of non-targeted profiling with molecular networking and targeted bioassay directly addresses the challenge of efficiently isolating novel bioactive compounds from complex plant extracts.

proposals.title: Elucidation and Heterologous Reconstitution of the Biosynthetic Pathway for Neuroprotective Lignans from Sedum kamtschaticum in Saccharomyces cerevisiae
proposals.motivation: Lignans, such as the prenylhydroquinone glycoside recently isolated by our group from Sedum kamtschaticum, exhibit promising neuroprotective activity against Alzheimer's disease-related tau pathology. However, their sustainable supply is limited by low natural abundance and complex extraction processes. Microbial biosynthesis offers a scalable and environmentally friendly alternative, but the complete biosynthetic pathway for these bioactive lignans remains unknown, hindering production efforts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the biosynthetic gene cluster for the neuroprotective prenylhydroquinone lignan in S. kamtschaticum can be identified and functionally reconstituted in a microbial host, enabling de novo production of the target compound and its analogs.
proposals.method: First, we will perform RNA sequencing of S. kamtschaticum leaves to identify candidate genes involved in the phenylpropanoid and subsequent lignan pathways, focusing on dirigent proteins, pinoresinol-lariciresinol reductases, and prenyltransferases. Candidate genes will be cloned and their enzymatic functions validated in vitro using substrates like coniferyl alcohol. The complete pathway will then be assembled and optimized in engineered Saccharomyces cerevisiae using Golden Gate assembly and modular vector systems, with metabolic flux balanced by tuning promoter strength and codon usage.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first elucidated biosynthetic pathway for a prenylhydroquinone lignan from Sedum species and establish a proof-of-concept microbial platform for its production. It will contribute a new genetic toolkit for lignan engineering and a sustainable source of the compound for further pharmacological evaluation against neurodegenerative targets.
proposals.novelty: While microbial production of simple lignans like pinoresinol has been reported, the pathway for complex, prenylated hydroquinone glycosides with specific bioactivity remains entirely unexplored. This proposal uniquely integrates phytochemical discovery from our previous work with advanced biosynthetic engineering to address a supply bottleneck for a novel neuroprotective agent.

proposals.title: A Microfluidic Droplet-Based Platform for Ultra-High-Throughput Screening of Plant Extracts for Antimelanogenic Activity
proposals.motivation: The discovery of natural product-based skin-lightening agents, such as methoxyflavones from Kaempferia parviflora, is limited by the low-throughput, reagent-intensive nature of conventional cell-based assays (e.g., B16F10 melanin content). Pre-2026, microfluidic droplet technology has revolutionized drug screening by enabling picoliter-scale reactions, but its application to plant extract libraries for specific bioactivities like melanogenesis inhibition remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that compartmentalizing individual B16F10 mouse melanoma cells with nanoliter volumes of plant extracts in microfluidic droplets will enable the quantitative, high-content screening of thousands of samples for antimelanogenic activity with drastically reduced sample and reagent consumption.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate a PDMS-based droplet microfluidic chip with integrated on-chip mixers to encapsulate single B16F10 cells, culture medium, and a unique plant extract per droplet. Post-encapsulation, droplets will be incubated off-chip. A fluorescence-based readout for intracellular melanin or tyrosinase activity (using established probes like L-DOPA auto-oxidation or fluorescent substrates) will be integrated. Droplets will be analyzed in-flow using a customized system coupling a laser-induced fluorescence detector to the chip outlet, allowing real-time sorting of active droplets for downstream compound identification via LC-MS.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, miniaturized screening platform that accelerates the discovery pipeline for natural antimelanogenic compounds. It will provide a methodological blueprint for applying microfluidic high-throughput screening to other plant-derived bioactivities. The project is expected to identify new lead compounds from previously screened or unexplored plant sources with higher efficiency.
proposals.novelty: While microfluidics has been used for cell assays and compound screening, the specific integration of a phenotypic melanogenesis inhibition assay in a droplet format for screening complex plant extract libraries is novel. This approach moves beyond bulk well-plate assays to a single-cell, ultra-high-throughput format tailored for natural product research.

proposals.title: Elucidating the Ecological Defense Function of Prenylated Lignans and Flavonoids in Source Plants: A Functional Metabolomics Approach
proposals.motivation: Ki Hyun Kim's recent work has successfully isolated and characterized novel bioactive lignans and methoxyflavones from plants like Sedum kamtschaticum and Kaempferia parviflora, demonstrating potent in vitro pharmacological activities (e.g., anti-tauopathy, anti-melanogenesis). However, the native, in planta ecological roles of these specialized metabolites remain largely speculative and unvalidated. Determining whether these compounds function in plant defense against biotic stressors is a critical gap, as it connects their chemical ecology to their discovered bioactivities and informs sustainable sourcing and bioprospecting strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: The prenylated hydroquinone glycosides and methoxyflavones isolated from S. kamtschaticum and K. parviflora are biosynthesized as part of the plant's innate immune and defense system, providing direct resistance against fungal pathogens and deterring herbivorous insects.
proposals.method: We will employ a functional metabolomics and bioassay-guided approach. First, the spatial and temporal accumulation of target lignans and flavones will be mapped using LC-MS/MS in plant tissues under control, herbivore-wounded, and fungal (e.g., Botrytis cinerea)-elicited conditions. Purified compounds will be tested in dual-culture plate assays against a panel of plant pathogenic fungi and in no-choice feeding assays using generalist insect herbivores (e.g., Spodoptera litura). Correlation analysis between metabolite induction patterns and bioactivity scores will be performed. Finally, gene expression analysis of key biosynthetic genes (e.g., prenyltransferases, O-methyltransferases) via qRT-PCR will link defense induction to compound biosynthesis.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly assign an ecological function to recently discovered, structurally unique natural products, moving beyond cataloging chemical structures and in vitro cell-based activity. It will provide empirical evidence for the plant defense hypothesis of these compounds, offering a deeper evolutionary rationale for their bioactivity. The results will establish a foundational ecological context for future pharmacological studies and potentially reveal new modes of action for natural product-based agrochemicals.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Kim's prior work focuses on isolation and human-disease-targeted activity, this proposal uniquely pivots to investigate the native biological function of these same compounds within the source organism, an understudied aspect for these specific metabolite classes. Integrating targeted metabolomics with ecological bioassays on the exact compounds previously isolated for pharmaceutical potential represents a novel synthesis of phytochemistry and chemical ecology.

proposals.title: Systematic Evaluation of Bioassay-Guided Fractionation Bias: A Comparative Metabolomics and Activity Profiling Study of Black Ginger (Kaempferia parviflora)
proposals.motivation: Bioassay-guided fractionation (BGF) is the cornerstone of natural product drug discovery, but it inherently prioritizes abundant, easily isolatable compounds. Pre-2026 evidence suggests that potent bioactivity can reside in minor metabolites, which are often overlooked or lost during fractionation due to dilution or instability. This potential bias limits the discovery pipeline and may explain why many isolated 'active' compounds show diminished effects compared to the original crude extract.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that conventional BGF of a pharmacologically active extract will fail to identify the most potent minor constituents, and that a parallel, comprehensive metabolomics-guided approach will reveal these missed bioactive compounds.
proposals.method: We will use Black Ginger (Kaempferia parviflora), a source of known methoxyflavones, as a model. The crude extract will be subjected to parallel workflows: 1) Standard BGF for melanogenesis inhibition in B16F10 cells. 2) Untargeted UPLC-QTOF-MS/MS metabolomic profiling of all fractions to map chemical diversity independent of bioassay results. Key minor ions not enriched in BGF-active pools will be prioritized. These will be isolated using targeted, small-scale HPLC-MS-guided purification and their bioactivity tested at equal molar concentrations against BGF-isolated major compounds.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide direct, comparative evidence for the limitations of BGF, offering a methodological critique grounded in empirical data. It will propose a complementary metabolomics-integrated workflow to mitigate bias, potentially uncovering novel, highly potent minor flavonoids or other chemical classes from Kaempferia parviflora with superior bioactivity.
proposals.novelty: While the limitation of BGF is acknowledged, systematic head-to-head comparisons quantifying the 'miss rate' for potent minor metabolites are rare. This study innovatively uses contemporary (pre-2026) metabolomics not just for dereplication, but as a primary discovery tool to actively challenge and complement the traditional bioassay-guided paradigm.

proposals.title: Systematic Solvent Polarity-Based Extraction of Populus × tomentiglandulosa for the Discovery of Overlooked Polar and Non-Polar Bioactive Phenolics
proposals.motivation: Methanol is a standard extraction solvent in natural product chemistry, but its intermediate polarity may fail to efficiently capture highly polar phenolic acids and glycosides or highly non-polar aglycones and prenylated phenolics. This methodological limitation could lead to a biased chemical profile and the loss of potentially significant bioactive compounds, as evidenced by the diverse phenolic structures (e.g., prenylhydroquinone glycosides, methoxyflavones) isolated in Dr. Kim's recent work. A comprehensive, polarity-guided approach is therefore necessary for a complete phytochemical inventory and accurate bioactivity assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: Sequential extraction of plant material with solvents of increasing polarity (e.g., hexane, ethyl acetate, methanol, and water) will yield distinct phenolic fractions containing unique compounds not present in a standard methanol extract, and these compounds will exhibit differential bioactivity profiles relevant to neurodegenerative or hyperpigmentation disorders.
proposals.method: Dried, powdered plant material (e.g., Populus × tomentiglandulosa bark/leaves) will undergo sequential exhaustive extraction using a Soxhlet apparatus or repeated maceration with solvents of increasing polarity: hexane, ethyl acetate, methanol, and finally water. Each resultant fraction will be concentrated and analyzed via HPLC-PDA-ESI-MS/MS to compare phenolic fingerprints against a standard methanol-only extract. Bioactive fractions will be subjected to bioassay-guided fractionation (e.g., assays for tau phosphorylation inhibition in Aβ1–42-treated SH-SY5Y cells or melanogenesis inhibition in B16F10 cells) using column chromatography (silica gel, Sephadex LH-20) and preparative HPLC to isolate novel or unreported compounds for structural elucidation by NMR and HRMS.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a more rigorous extraction protocol for phenolic discovery, likely leading to the isolation of novel polar phenolic glycosides and non-polar aglycones. It will directly test the completeness of common extraction methods and provide a more comprehensive chemical basis for the observed pharmacological properties of the studied plants, potentially identifying new lead compounds with enhanced activity against tau pathology or melanogenesis.
proposals.novelty: While sequential extraction is known, its systematic application to specifically uncover phenolic compounds missed by routine methanol extraction in Dr. Kim's target plant species, coupled with targeted bioassays from his recent work, represents a deliberate strategy to fill a known methodological gap in natural product research.

proposals.title: Synergistic Anti-Neuroinflammatory Effects of Compound Mixtures from Sedum kamtschaticum: Deconstructing the Bioactivity of the Crude Extract
proposals.motivation: In natural product drug discovery, a persistent challenge is the frequent observation that isolated, purified compounds exhibit significantly lower bioactivity than the original crude extract. This discrepancy, often termed the "natural product synergy" problem, suggests that therapeutic effects may rely on complex compound interactions. Investigating these interactions is crucial for developing more effective, multi-target botanical therapeutics, particularly for complex diseases like Alzheimer's, where single-target drugs have shown limited success.
proposals.hypothesis: The superior anti-neuroinflammatory and tauopathy-modulating activity observed in the crude extract of Sedum kamtschaticum is not due to a single dominant compound (like the previously isolated prenylhydroquinone glycoside) but results from synergistic interactions between multiple, co-occurring bioactive constituents.
proposals.method: Following the bioassay-guided fractionation of S. kamtschaticum leaf extract, previously isolated individual compounds (e.g., the prenylhydroquinone glycoside, flavonoids, phenolic acids) will be systematically recombined into defined mixtures based on their natural relative abundances. Their effects on Aβ1–42-induced neuroinflammation (NO, PGE2, TNF-α, IL-1β in BV-2 microglia) and hyperphosphorylated tau production (in SH-SY5Y cells) will be quantified. Synergy will be evaluated using pre-2026 models: the Combination Index (CI) method of Chou-Talalay for dose-effect analysis and response surface methodology (RSM) to model multi-compound interactions and identify optimal synergistic ratios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic explanation for the bioactivity gap between crude extracts and isolated compounds, specifically for S. kamtschaticum. It will deliver a validated, synergistic multi-compound formulation with defined ratios, offering a more translatable natural product-based intervention strategy for Alzheimer's disease pathology. The methodological framework will serve as a model for deconstructing synergistic effects in other medicinal plants.
proposals.novelty: While synergy is often hypothesized, this proposal moves beyond simple pairwise combinations to systematically reconstruct and mathematically model the synergistic contribution of a full suite of co-isolated compounds from a single source, directly linking the phytochemical complexity of a specific extract to its observed in vitro efficacy.

proposals.title: Elucidating the Biosynthetic Pathway of Novel Anti-Neuroinflammatory Neolignans in Populus × tomentiglandulosa
proposals.motivation: The research group's prior phytochemical exploration of Populus × tomentiglandulosa has led to the isolation of novel neolignans and phenolic compounds with promising anti-neuroinflammatory and anti-Alzheimer's disease (AD) bioactivities. As of 2025, the biosynthetic origins of these specific, bioactive neolignan scaffolds in this plant species remain completely unknown. Understanding their biosynthesis is a critical step towards sustainable production, structural diversification via synthetic biology, and ultimately, the development of new lead compounds for neurodegenerative diseases.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the novel neolignans in P. × tomentiglandulosa are synthesized via a specific, yet uncharacterized, branch of the phenylpropanoid pathway involving unique regiospecific oxidative coupling of monolignol precursors and subsequent tailoring modifications such as glycosylation or prenylation.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-omics approach, integrating transcriptomics and metabolomics. First, RNA will be extracted from different plant tissues (bark, leaves) and sequenced (Illumina NovaSeq) to assemble a de novo transcriptome. Candidate biosynthetic genes (e.g., dirigent proteins, laccases, cytochrome P450s, glycosyltransferases, prenyltransferases) will be identified via homology-based screening and co-expression analysis with the target neolignan metabolites, quantified via LC-MS/MS. The function of top candidate enzymes will be validated through heterologous expression in Nicotiana benthamiana or yeast, followed by feeding assays with proposed precursors and LC-MS analysis of products.
proposals.contribution: This work will map the first dedicated biosynthetic pathway for these structurally unique, bioactive neolignans, directly linking genotype to chemotype in P. × tomentiglandulosa. It will provide a genetic toolkit (enzymes and genes) for the biotechnological production of these compounds. Furthermore, it will expand fundamental knowledge of metabolic diversity within the phenylpropanoid pathway in the Populus genus.
proposals.novelty: While neolignan biosynthesis is studied in some plants, the specific pathway for the unreported anti-neuroinflammatory neolignans from this hybrid poplar is entirely novel. The proposal uniquely combines the group's expertise in isolating bioactive natural products with contemporary plant multi-omics to move from compound discovery to pathway elucidation.

proposals.title: Synthesis, Biological Evaluation, and Structure-Activity Relationship of Surinamensinol A and B Analogues Targeting Neurodegenerative Tau Pathology
proposals.motivation: Neolignans like surinamensinol A and B, isolated from natural sources, show promising but preliminary bioactivity. Establishing their structure-activity relationships (SAR) is crucial for identifying lead compounds against neurodegenerative diseases, particularly those involving tau protein aggregation, which remains a major therapeutic challenge pre-2026. Systematic chemical modification of these scaffolds can pinpoint key pharmacophores for enhanced potency and selectivity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific modifications to the phenolic hydroxyl groups, methoxy substitutions, and the dihydrobenzofuran core of surinamensinol neolignans are critical determinants of their ability to inhibit the production of hyperphosphorylated tau in neuronal cells.
proposals.method: We will employ semi-synthesis and total synthesis to generate a focused library of surinamensinol analogues, systematically modifying the parent structures through acetylation, methylation, and selective hydrogenation. All compounds will be characterized using NMR and HRMS. Their bioactivity will be evaluated in Aβ1–42-treated SH-SY5Y cells, measuring levels of hyperphosphorylated tau (e.g., p-Tau Ser396) via western blot, following the model established in the researcher's prior work on Sedum kamtschaticum. Dose-response curves will be generated to determine IC50 values.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first detailed SAR map for surinamensinol-type neolignans against tau pathology, identifying the minimal structural requirements for activity. It will deliver new, synthetically accessible chemical probes for studying tau-related neurodegeneration and potentially advance a novel chemotype for further drug development.
proposals.novelty: While surinamensinol A and B have been isolated, their systematic chemical modification and biological evaluation against a specific tau-related target represent an unexplored avenue, moving beyond mere compound isolation to rational design based on this unique neolignan scaffold.

proposals.title: Development of a Scalable Process for the Kilogram-Scale Isolation of Prenylhydroquinone Glycosides from Sedum kamtschaticum for Preclinical Alzheimer's Disease Studies
proposals.motivation: The prenylhydroquinone glycoside from Sedum kamtschaticum has demonstrated promising activity against hyperphosphorylated tau in an in vitro Alzheimer's disease model, but its therapeutic potential is hindered by the lack of a scalable isolation process. Current natural product drug discovery faces a critical 'valley of death' between initial bioactive compound discovery and preclinical development, primarily due to inefficient scale-up. Establishing a robust, kilogram-scale purification protocol is essential to produce sufficient material for comprehensive in vivo efficacy and safety testing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by systematically optimizing solvent systems, chromatographic supports (e.g., HP-20 resin), and employing counter-current chromatography (CCC), we can develop a cost-effective and scalable isolation process that yields multi-gram quantities of the target prenylhydroquinone glycoside with >95% purity, suitable for pharmaceutical development.
proposals.method: The method begins with a Design of Experiments (DoE) approach to optimize the initial extraction parameters (solvent, temperature, time) for maximum yield. The crude extract will then be subjected to a scalable solid-phase extraction using Diaion HP-20 resin to enrich the target compound. Final purification will be achieved using industrial-scale high-performance counter-current chromatography (HPCCC) with a solvent system optimized based on the compound's partition coefficient (K). Process efficiency will be monitored by HPLC, and the structure of bulk isolates will be confirmed by NMR and HRMS.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first reported scalable isolation protocol for a bioactive prenylhydroquinone glycoside from Sedum kamtschaticum, directly enabling advanced preclinical studies. It will establish a methodological framework that can be applied to other promising but scarce natural products, bridging a key gap between academic discovery and industrial pharmaceutical development.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Kim's prior work isolated and characterized this novel compound on a milligram scale, this proposal focuses on the non-trivial engineering challenge of scaling the process for pharmaceutical supply, integrating DoE and industrial-scale HPCCC specifically for this compound class, which has not been reported pre-2026.

proposals.title: Microfluidic On-Chip Extraction and Bioactivity Screening of Anti-Melanogenic Compounds from Rare Plant Species
proposals.motivation: Traditional natural product discovery requires large quantities of plant material for solvent extraction and fractionation, which is unsustainable for rare or endangered species and limits high-throughput screening. Microfluidic platforms, as of pre-2026, have demonstrated potential for miniaturized chemical processing but have not been widely integrated into the initial discovery pipeline for novel plant-derived bioactive compounds. This approach addresses the need for efficient, low-consumption methods to explore phytochemical diversity from limited botanical sources.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a purpose-designed microfluidic chip can perform integrated solid-liquid extraction, partial purification, and real-time bioactivity analysis of plant extracts, enabling the identification of anti-melanogenic or neuroprotective compounds from milligram quantities of rare plant tissue.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate a polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS)-based microfluidic chip with integrated serpentine mixing channels and membrane-based filtration zones. The chip will be coupled to an HPLC-MS system via a microfluidic interface for parallel chemical analysis. Fresh or freeze-dried plant material (e.g., from *Sedum* or *Kaempferia* species) will be homogenized and loaded onto the chip. On-chip extraction will be performed using miniaturized pressurized solvent flow (e.g., ethanol/water). The crude extract will then be directly introduced into a parallel on-chip cell culture chamber containing B16F10 mouse melanoma cells for real-time melanogenesis inhibition assays, monitored via integrated optical detection for melanin content.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept platform that drastically reduces the sample and solvent requirements for the initial discovery of plant bioactive compounds. It will contribute a novel methodological workflow to natural products research, enabling the study of phytochemicals from rare or minimally available plant specimens. The successful integration of extraction and bioassay on a single chip represents a significant step towards automated, high-content natural product screening.
proposals.novelty: While microfluidics has been used for cell-based assays or chemical synthesis, its application for the integrated, on-chip extraction and immediate biological testing of compounds directly from minute amounts of complex plant tissue is a novel and non-obvious convergence of natural product chemistry and lab-on-a-chip technology pre-2026.

proposals.title: Stability Profiling and Degradation Pathway Elucidation of Bioactive Prenylhydroquinone Glycosides from Sedum kamtschaticum Under Simulated Physiological and Storage Conditions
proposals.motivation: The discovery of novel bioactive natural products, such as the unreported prenylhydroquinone glycoside from Sedum kamtschaticum, is a primary focus in drug discovery. However, the therapeutic potential and practical utility of these compounds are critically dependent on their chemical stability, which is often overlooked. Understanding degradation pathways is essential for assessing in vivo efficacy, ensuring reliable bioassay results, and developing stable formulations, yet systematic stability studies for newly isolated compounds remain scarce in the literature.
proposals.hypothesis: The newly isolated prenylhydroquinone glycoside from Sedum kamtschaticum will undergo specific, condition-dependent degradation (e.g., hydrolysis, oxidation) to yield identifiable breakdown products, some of which may retain or modulate the original anti-tau hyperphosphorylation activity.
proposals.method: The isolated prenylhydroquinone glycoside will be subjected to forced degradation studies under controlled conditions of pH, temperature, light, and oxidative stress, simulating physiological and storage environments. Degradation will be monitored over time using HPLC-PDA and UPLC-QTOF-MS/MS. Major degradation products will be isolated via preparative HPLC and structurally elucidated using NMR spectroscopy (1D and 2D). The biological activity (effect on hyperphosphorylated tau in Aβ1–42-treated SH-SY5Y cells) of the parent compound and key degradation products will be comparatively evaluated.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first stability profile and degradation pathway for a novel class of neuroactive prenylhydroquinone glycosides. It will deliver critical data for future pharmacokinetic studies and formulation development, while also identifying potentially active or inactive metabolites that could explain in vivo outcomes. The methodology will serve as a model for post-isolation characterization of natural products.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Kim's prior work focuses on the isolation and initial bioactivity screening of novel compounds, this proposal shifts the paradigm to investigate their chemical fate post-isolation, a critical but underexplored step in natural product research that bridges discovery and development.

proposals.title: In Silico Prioritization of Bioactive Natural Products from Korean Medicinal Plants: Structure-Based Virtual Screening Against Tau Kinase and Melanogenesis-Related Targets
proposals.motivation: Natural product discovery remains resource-intensive, requiring laborious isolation and bioassay-guided fractionation. Pre-2026, computational methods like molecular docking offer a powerful, cost-effective strategy to prioritize compounds for experimental validation, accelerating the identification of novel bioactive leads. This approach is particularly valuable for targets implicated in neurodegenerative diseases like Alzheimer's (e.g., tau kinases) and hyperpigmentation disorders.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that computational docking of a library of previously isolated natural compounds from our lab's repository against specific protein targets (e.g., GSK-3β for tau phosphorylation, tyrosinase for melanogenesis) will accurately predict in vitro bioactivity, enabling the rational selection of high-priority candidates for confirmatory assays.
proposals.method: We will construct a 3D compound library of 50-100 natural products previously isolated in our lab (e.g., from Populus, Sedum, Kaempferia). Using AutoDock Vina or Glide (Schrödinger Suite), we will perform rigid and flexible docking against the crystal structures of target proteins (GSK-3β: PDB 1Q3D; tyrosinase: PDB 5M8Q). Docking poses will be scored, and the top-ranked compounds will be evaluated for binding mode stability via molecular dynamics simulations using GROMACS (pre-2026 versions).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a validated in silico pipeline for pre-screening natural product libraries, directly linking our lab's isolation expertise with computational pharmacology. It will identify new, predicted bioactivities for known compounds, potentially revealing multi-target agents and providing a rationale for targeted isolation of analogs. The results will be a proof-of-concept for rational, efficiency-driven natural product research.
proposals.novelty: While docking is common, its systematic application to prioritize a bespoke library of compounds already isolated and characterized in-house for novel target associations (e.g., Sedum compounds against tau kinases) is novel. This bridges the gap between pure computational screening and our established wet-lab workflow in a targeted feedback loop.

proposals.title: In Vivo Evaluation of Butein, a Natural Chalcone, for the Treatment of Diet-Induced Obesity: Efficacy, Safety, and Mechanistic Insights
proposals.motivation: Obesity is a global metabolic disorder with limited long-term therapeutic options and significant side effects from current drugs. Natural products, particularly chalcones like butein, have shown promising in vitro anti-adipogenic and anti-inflammatory activities relevant to obesity. However, comprehensive in vivo validation of butein's efficacy and safety in established obesity models is lacking, which is a critical step for translational development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that oral administration of butein will ameliorate diet-induced obesity in mice by reducing body weight gain, improving metabolic parameters, and modulating key pathways in adipose tissue inflammation and lipid metabolism without significant toxicity.
proposals.method: C57BL/6 mice will be fed a high-fat diet (HFD) for 8 weeks to induce obesity, followed by 6 weeks of co-treatment with butein (10, 30 mg/kg/day, p.o.) or vehicle. Body weight and food intake will be monitored weekly. At termination, we will analyze adipose tissue mass, serum lipid profiles, glucose tolerance, and liver enzymes. Tissues will be subjected to histological analysis (H&E staining) and molecular profiling via western blot (for p-AMPK, SIRT1, NF-κB) and qPCR (for adipogenic and inflammatory markers like PPARγ, TNF-α). Acute oral toxicity will be assessed in a separate cohort per OECD guidelines.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive in vivo efficacy and safety profile of butein for obesity, establishing a foundational preclinical dataset. It will elucidate the compound's mechanism of action in a whole-organism context, identifying key molecular targets in adipose and liver tissues, thereby advancing butein as a candidate for further anti-obesity drug development.
proposals.novelty: While butein's in vitro bioactivity is documented, its specific anti-obesity effects in a chronic in vivo HFD model remain unexplored. This proposal uniquely integrates efficacy assessment with detailed mechanistic toxicology and molecular pathway analysis, bridging the gap between cell-based findings and potential therapeutic application.

proposals.title: Genome-Mining-Driven Discovery of Natalamycin Analogs from Actinobacterial Strains and Evaluation of Their Anti-Tau and Neuroprotective Bioactivities
proposals.motivation: Traditional bioassay-guided fractionation is time- and resource-intensive, often rediscovering known compounds. Pre-2026, genome mining has emerged as a powerful tool to predict biosynthetic potential, but its application to target specific, structurally intriguing scaffolds like natalamycin (a spirotetronate macrolide with reported bioactivity) for prioritized isolation remains underexplored. This project aims to invert the discovery pipeline to efficiently uncover novel chemical entities with potential relevance to neurodegenerative targets, aligning with the researcher's expertise in isolating bioactive natural products.
proposals.hypothesis: By first mining actinobacterial genomes for biosynthetic gene clusters (BGCs) homologous to the natalamycin cluster, we can prioritize strains that are genetically predisposed to produce novel natalamycin analogs, which will then exhibit measurable bioactivity against pathological tau protein aggregation and offer neuroprotective effects.
proposals.method: We will utilize antiSMASH (version 6.0 or earlier) and PRISM 4 to screen in-house and public actinobacterial genomes for BGCs containing conserved ketosynthase domains and spirotetronate-specific genes from the natalamycin model. Strains with high-scoring, divergent BGCs will be cultured, and LC-MS/MS-based metabolomics with GNPS molecular networking will guide the targeted isolation of predicted analogs. Purified compounds will be tested in Aβ1–42-treated SH-SY5Y cells for reduction of hyperphosphorylated tau (as in the researcher's prior work) and in B16F10 cells for anti-melanogenesis as a secondary bioactivity screen.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a streamlined, genomics-first framework for natural product discovery, directly yielding new natalamycin-like chemical entities. It will provide insight into the structure-activity relationships of spirotetronates against tau pathology and potentially identify novel leads for neurodegenerative disease research.
proposals.novelty: The approach reverses the conventional workflow by using genomic prediction as the primary filter for strain selection, specifically targeting the natalamycin scaffold, rather than employing broad bioactivity screens on random extracts. This integrates targeted genome mining with specific phenotypic assays relevant to the researcher's established models.

proposals.title: Expanding the Bioactivity Profile of Natalamycin A: Systematic Evaluation of Anticancer and Antibacterial Activities Against Drug-Resistant Pathogens
proposals.motivation: Natalamycin A, a polyene macrolide isolated from Streptomyces sp., has been primarily characterized for its antifungal properties. Given the structural complexity and known bioactivity of related polyenes, there is a significant opportunity to explore its potential against other pressing therapeutic targets, particularly drug-resistant cancers and bacterial infections, which remain major global health challenges. Systematic evaluation of its off-target pharmacological effects could reveal new therapeutic applications and structure-activity relationships for this compound class.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that natalamycin A and its structurally related analogs possess significant, previously unreported anticancer and antibacterial activities, potentially mediated through mechanisms distinct from its known antifungal action.
proposals.method: Natalamycin A and available analogs will be isolated or sourced for bioactivity screening. Anticancer potential will be assessed via MTT assays against a panel of human cancer cell lines (e.g., A549, MCF-7, HepG2, and drug-resistant variants), with flow cytometry used to analyze apoptosis and cell cycle arrest. Antibacterial activity will be tested against a range of Gram-positive (e.g., MRSA) and Gram-negative (e.g., E. coli, P. aeruginosa) strains using broth microdilution to determine MICs. Preliminary mechanistic studies will involve proteomic analysis (LC-MS/MS) of treated bacterial and cancer cells to identify potential protein targets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive dataset on the anticancer and antibacterial activities of natalamycin A, expanding its pharmacological profile. It will contribute to the field of natural product drug discovery by identifying new lead structures for combating multidrug-resistant pathogens and cancers, and establish a foundation for subsequent SAR studies to optimize bioactivity.
proposals.novelty: While natalamycin A's antifungal activity is documented, its systematic evaluation against cancer cells and clinically relevant drug-resistant bacteria is unexplored. This proposal moves beyond the compound's initial screening context to investigate its broader chemotherapeutic potential, a non-obvious direction given its classification as a polyene antifungal agent.

proposals.title: Elucidating Environmental Degradation Pathways and Stability of Natalamycin A in a Model Termite Nest Ecosystem
proposals.motivation: Natalamycin A, a potent antifungal polyketide isolated from *Streptomyces* sp. in a termite nest, represents a promising natural product scaffold. However, its ecological function and persistence in its native environment are unknown. Understanding its degradation is crucial for assessing its ecological role, environmental fate, and for informing the development of stable analogs for potential applications.
proposals.hypothesis: Natalamycin A undergoes specific biotic and/or abiotic degradation in the termite nest ecosystem, mediated by microbial activity, nest materials, and environmental factors, leading to identifiable transformation products that are less bioactive.
proposals.method: We will simulate a termite nest ecosystem using sterilized and non-sterilized nest materials. Natalamycin A will be incubated under controlled conditions (temperature, humidity, pH) mimicking the nest. Samples will be extracted at timed intervals and analyzed via HPLC-PDA and LC-MS/MS (Q-TOF) to monitor parent compound depletion and identify degradation products. High-resolution MS and NMR will be used for structural elucidation of major transformation products. Bioactivity of key degradants will be assessed against *Candida albicans* to correlate structural change with functional loss.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first map of natalamycin A's environmental degradation, identifying key labile sites in its structure. It will offer insights into the natural lifecycle of a termite-associated antibiotic and generate critical stability data to guide future semi-synthetic optimization for enhanced durability.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Kim's prior work focuses on the isolation and bioactivity of novel compounds, this proposal inverts the paradigm to study the stability and degradation of a known bioactive natural product within its specific ecological context, a perspective rarely applied to termite nest-derived metabolites.

proposals.title: Disentangling Antioxidant and Glucose Uptake Mechanisms: Isolation and Evaluation of Bioactive Compounds from Natural Sources for Type 2 Diabetes Management
proposals.motivation: Oxidative stress is a key pathological factor in Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (T2DM), and many natural products with antidiabetic properties exhibit strong antioxidant activity. However, it remains unclear whether their primary therapeutic mechanism is direct antioxidant action, enhancement of cellular glucose uptake, or a synergistic combination. Disentangling these mechanisms is crucial for rational drug design and understanding the true bioactive principles of medicinal plants.
proposals.hypothesis: The primary antidiabetic mechanism of selected natural product-derived compounds is their direct antioxidant activity, which mitigates oxidative stress in insulin-resistant cells, rather than a direct stimulation of cellular glucose uptake pathways.
proposals.method: We will isolate pure compounds from promising sources like Black Ginger (Kaempferia parviflora) using bioassay-guided fractionation (silica gel, Sephadex LH-20, preparative HPLC). Their antioxidant capacity will be quantified via DPPH, ABTS, and ORAC assays. Concurrently, glucose uptake will be measured in insulin-resistant HepG2 and L6 myotube cells using 2-NBDG fluorescence. Key experiments will involve pre-treating cells with a potent antioxidant (e.g., N-acetylcysteine) or an antioxidant enzyme inhibitor to see if it modulates the compound's effect on glucose uptake, and using ROS-sensitive fluorescent probes (DCFH-DA) to correlate intracellular ROS reduction with metabolic improvement.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a clear mechanistic framework for classifying natural antidiabetic agents based on their primary mode of action. It will identify specific lead compounds where antioxidant effect is the dominant mechanism, offering candidates for mitigating diabetes-associated oxidative damage. The findings will advance the field beyond simple activity screening to mechanistic phytopharmacology.
proposals.novelty: While many studies report both antioxidant and antidiabetic activities, this proposal systematically isolates and tests these mechanisms against each other in the same experimental system using specific pharmacological modulators, moving from correlation to causation.

proposals.title: Rapid Dereplication of Quinic Acid Derivatives in Plant Extracts via Computed NMR Database Matching for Accelerated Discovery of Anti-Neuroinflammatory Agents
proposals.motivation: The discovery of bioactive natural products, such as anti-neuroinflammatory agents, is bottlenecked by the slow, labor-intensive process of isolating and structurally elucidating known compounds from complex plant extracts. Quinic acid derivatives, prevalent in plants like *Populus* and *Sedum* species studied by the PI, exhibit diverse bioactivities but are challenging to identify quickly in mixtures. Pre-2026, computed NMR databases have emerged, yet their application for rapid, targeted screening of specific phytochemical classes in crude extracts remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: That a curated library of computed 1H and 13C NMR chemical shifts for known quinic acid derivatives can enable their rapid and accurate identification in crude plant extracts via spectral database matching, bypassing initial isolation steps for known compounds.
proposals.method: We will first construct a focused database by calculating NMR chemical shifts (1H, 13C) for a library of known quinic acid derivatives using density functional theory (DFT) at the B3LYP/6-311+G(d,p) level with the GIAO method, referencing experimental data from literature. Crude extracts from plants like *Sedum kamtschaticum* will be analyzed via 1D and 2D NMR (HSQC, HMBC). Spectral matching will be performed using an in-house algorithm that compares experimental peaks against the computed database, employing tolerance windows and scoring based on chemical shift similarity and correlation pattern consistency to propose candidate structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a streamlined dereplication pipeline specifically for quinic acid derivatives, significantly accelerating the early-stage screening of plant extracts. It will directly contribute to the PI's research on anti-neuroinflammatory and tau-related bioactivity by enabling the rapid prioritization of extracts containing novel or potent analogues. The method will serve as a model for applying computed NMR libraries to other classes of natural products.
proposals.novelty: While computed NMR is used for structure elucidation of pure compounds, its targeted application as a pre-isolation screening tool for a specific, bioactive compound class in complex mixtures is novel. This approach integrates computational chemistry directly into the initial phytochemical workflow of natural product discovery.

proposals.title: Synergistic Bioactivity of Crude Extracts and Simplified Fractions from Medicinal Plants: A Comparative Study with Purified Compounds for Neuroprotection and Anti-Melanogenesis
proposals.motivation: Traditional natural product research prioritizes the isolation of pure compounds, potentially overlooking the complex synergistic interactions present in crude extracts. In a pre-2026 context, there is growing recognition that the multi-target effects of phytochemical mixtures may offer superior therapeutic efficacy for complex diseases like Alzheimer's or hyperpigmentation disorders compared to single agents. This study addresses the need to systematically evaluate whether simplified, bioactive fractions retain the beneficial synergy of whole extracts while offering more practical standardization than pure compounds.
proposals.hypothesis: Crude extracts and semi-purified fractions from selected medicinal plants (e.g., Sedum kamtschaticum, Kaempferia parviflora) will exhibit greater in vitro bioactivity (anti-tau hyperphosphorylation, anti-melanogenesis) than their individual purified constituents, due to synergistic interactions among co-occurring compounds.
proposals.method: Plant materials (Sedum kamtschaticum leaves, Kaempferia parviflora rhizomes) will be sequentially extracted with solvents of increasing polarity. Extracts will be fractionated via vacuum liquid chromatography (VLC) into simplified, chemically distinct fractions. Bioactive pure compounds (e.g., prenylhydroquinone glycosides, methoxyflavones) will be isolated from active fractions using preparative HPLC, as per the researcher's established protocols. All samples (crude extracts, VLC fractions, pure compounds) will be tested in parallel using standardized cell-based assays: Aβ1–42-treated SH-SY5Y cells for tau hyperphosphorylation and B16F10 cells for melanogenesis inhibition. Dose-response curves and combination index (CI) analysis via the Chou-Talalay method will quantify synergy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence for the therapeutic value of phytochemical mixtures, challenging the pure-compound-centric paradigm in natural product drug discovery. It will identify specific plant sources and fractionation stages where synergistic bioactivity is maximized, offering a practical framework for developing more effective, multi-target botanical preparations or nutraceuticals.
proposals.novelty: While synergy is often assumed, this study directly and quantitatively compares the bioactivity trajectory from crude extract to pure compound within the same experimental system, using the researcher's own previously isolated compounds as benchmarks, which is not commonly done.

proposals.title: In Vivo Antitumor Efficacy of Prenylated Phenolic Compounds from Sedum kamtschaticum in Murine Xenograft Models
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in cancer therapy, there remains a critical need for novel, less toxic chemotherapeutic agents. Natural products, particularly prenylated phenolics, are a rich source of bioactive compounds with promising in vitro anticancer activity, but their in vivo efficacy is often underexplored. Validating these compounds in relevant animal models is a crucial step in the drug discovery pipeline.
proposals.hypothesis: The prenylhydroquinone glycoside and related methoxyflavones (compounds 4-6, 9, and 10) previously isolated and shown to have in vitro bioactivity will exhibit significant antitumor effects in vivo by inhibiting tumor growth in mouse xenograft models without inducing severe systemic toxicity.
proposals.method: We will establish subcutaneous xenograft models in immunodeficient mice using human cancer cell lines relevant to the compounds' in vitro targets (e.g., SH-SY5Y neuroblastoma or other solid tumor lines). Compounds 4-6, 9, and 10 will be administered intraperitoneally at optimized doses based on preliminary toxicity studies. Tumor volume will be measured biweekly, and mice will be monitored for weight loss and clinical signs. At endpoint, tumors will be excised, weighed, and analyzed via immunohistochemistry for proliferation (Ki-67) and apoptosis (cleaved caspase-3) markers. Pharmacokinetic analysis of the lead compound will be performed using LC-MS/MS.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first in vivo validation of the antitumor potential of specific prenylated compounds from Sedum kamtschaticum and Kaempferia parviflora. It will identify a lead candidate for further development and offer preliminary data on its mechanism of action and therapeutic window in a living system.
proposals.novelty: While the isolation and in vitro neuroprotective or anti-melanogenic effects of these compounds have been reported, their direct in vivo antitumor efficacy against established tumors has not been investigated. This proposal bridges the gap between phytochemical isolation and preclinical cancer pharmacology for these specific natural products.

proposals.title: Elucidating the Molecular Targets and Downstream NF-κB/MAPK Signaling Pathways for Nitric Oxide Inhibition by Novel Prenylhydroquinone and Methoxyflavone Derivatives in LPS-Stimulated BV2 Microglia
proposals.motivation: Chronic neuroinflammation, driven by activated microglia, is a key pathological feature in neurodegenerative diseases like Alzheimer's. While natural compounds show promise in suppressing inflammatory mediators like nitric oxide (NO), their precise molecular targets and signaling cascades in microglia remain poorly defined, limiting therapeutic development. Elucidating these mechanisms for promising lead compounds is a critical step in the field.
proposals.hypothesis: Compounds 5 and 10, identified from prior screening, inhibit LPS-induced NO production in microglia by selectively targeting upstream kinases (e.g., TAK1, Src) or directly modulating the IKK complex, thereby suppressing the canonical NF-κB and MAPK signaling pathways.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of molecular docking (using AutoDock Vina) to predict binding affinities to key inflammatory targets (IKKβ, TAK1, Src) and in vitro validation in LPS-stimulated BV2 microglia. Target engagement will be confirmed via cellular thermal shift assay (CETSA). Downstream effects will be analyzed by western blotting for phospho-proteins (p-IκBα, p-p65, p-p38, p-JNK, p-ERK) and nuclear translocation of p65 (immunofluorescence). Specific pathway inhibition will be tested using selective activators and siRNA knockdown of predicted targets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a detailed mechanistic map of how these specific natural product-derived compounds modulate microglial activation, moving beyond simple phenotypic screening. It will identify novel, pharmacologically relevant targets within neuroinflammatory pathways and establish a structure-activity relationship framework for optimizing anti-inflammatory leads for neurodegenerative diseases.
proposals.novelty: While many natural products inhibit NO, the specific targeting of prenylhydroquinone glycosides (like those from Sedum) and methoxyflavones (from Kaempferia) towards discrete nodes in microglial signaling networks is unexplored. This proposal integrates computational prediction with rigorous biochemical validation to deconvolute their mechanism, a non-obvious approach given the typical complexity of natural product effects.

proposals.title: Elucidating the Mechanistic Link Between Anti-inflammatory and Antiproliferative Activities of Prenylhydroquinone Glycosides and Methoxyflavones in Cancer Cell Models
proposals.motivation: Chronic inflammation is a well-established hallmark of cancer, driving tumor initiation, promotion, and progression. Many natural product-derived compounds exhibit both anti-inflammatory and antitumor activities, but it remains unclear whether these effects are independent or mechanistically connected. Establishing a causal link is crucial for developing more targeted chemopreventive and therapeutic agents from natural sources.
proposals.hypothesis: The anti-inflammatory activity of specific prenylhydroquinone glycosides (e.g., from Sedum kamtschaticum) and methoxyflavones (e.g., from Kaempferia parviflora) directly contributes to their observed antiproliferative effects in cancer cells by suppressing key pro-tumorigenic inflammatory signaling pathways.
proposals.method: We will isolate compounds 5 and 10 from their respective plant sources and confirm structures via NMR and LC-MS. Their anti-inflammatory activity will be quantified in LPS-stimulated RAW 264.7 macrophages by measuring inhibition of NO, PGE2, and pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6) via Griess assay, ELISA, and qRT-PCR. Concurrently, antiproliferative effects will be assessed in relevant cancer cell lines (e.g., B16F10, SH-SY5Y) using MTT assays. The mechanistic link will be tested by: 1) Correlating anti-inflammatory potency (IC50 for cytokine suppression) with antiproliferative IC50 across a panel of structural analogs. 2) Using siRNA knockdown or specific pharmacological inhibitors (e.g., against NF-κB or STAT3) to determine if blocking the inflammatory pathway attenuates the compounds' antiproliferative effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide direct experimental evidence for or against a causal relationship between anti-inflammatory and antitumor effects for two important classes of natural compounds. It will identify specific inflammatory mediators (e.g., COX-2, iNOS, specific cytokines) most critically linked to the antiproliferative outcome, offering new molecular targets for natural product-based drug discovery in cancer chemoprevention.
proposals.novelty: While Ki Hyun Kim's prior work isolated these bioactive compounds and demonstrated separate anti-inflammatory or antiproliferative activities, this proposal uniquely focuses on experimentally dissecting the causal mechanistic link between these two bioactivities within the same cellular models, moving beyond correlation to establish causation.

proposals.title: Untargeted LC-MS Metabolomics for Comprehensive Polyphenol Profiling of Populus × tomentiglandulosa Bark Extract and Correlation with Anti-Neuroinflammatory Activity
proposals.motivation: Traditional bioassay-guided fractionation, while effective for isolating major bioactive compounds, risks overlooking minor or synergistic polyphenols critical for full extract efficacy. This is particularly relevant for neuroinflammation, a complex pathological process in Alzheimer's disease where multi-target modulation by polyphenol mixtures is hypothesized to be beneficial. A comprehensive chemical profile is therefore a prerequisite for understanding the complete pharmacological basis of natural extracts like those from Populus × tomentiglandulosa, which your prior work has shown to be a source of bioactive compounds.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that untargeted metabolomics will reveal a significantly broader spectrum of polyphenols in P. tomentiglandulosa bark extract than previously identified through guided isolation, and that specific, previously unreported metabolite clusters will correlate strongly with in vitro anti-neuroinflammatory activity in microglial cells.
proposals.method: We will employ ultra-high-performance liquid chromatography coupled with quadrupole time-of-flight mass spectrometry (UHPLC-QTOF-MS) for untargeted profiling of the polyphenol-rich extract. Data-dependent acquisition (DDA) will be used to collect MS/MS spectra for feature annotation. Raw data will be processed using XCMS Online for peak picking, alignment, and annotation against public databases (e.g., GNPS, MassBank) and an in-house library of known polyphenols. Multivariate statistical analysis (PCA, OPLS-DA) using MetaboAnalyst 5.0 will correlate metabolomic profiles with bioactivity data from LPS-induced BV-2 microglial cells, measuring TNF-α and IL-6 suppression.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive metabolomic map of P. tomentiglandulosa bark, establishing a foundational chemical dataset for this species. It will directly link specific polyphenol signatures to a relevant biological endpoint (anti-neuroinflammation), moving beyond single-compounder characterization to a polypharmacology perspective. The generated data will guide future targeted isolation of novel active compounds and support standardization of the extract.
proposals.novelty: While metabolomics is established in natural products research, its application to specifically deconvolute the complex polyphenol profile of P. tomentiglandulosa and directly correlate it with anti-neuroinflammatory activity represents a novel, systems-level approach beyond your prior isolation-focused studies on this species.

proposals.title: Scalable Semi-Synthesis of the Prenylhydroquinone Glycoside from Sedum kamtschaticum: Enabling Preclinical Development of a Novel Anti-Alzheimer's Lead
proposals.motivation: In prior work, a novel prenylhydroquinone glycoside (compound 10) isolated from Sedum kamtschaticum demonstrated significant reduction of hyperphosphorylated tau in Aβ1–42-treated SH-SY5Y cells, positioning it as a promising multi-target anti-Alzheimer's lead. However, its development is hindered by low natural abundance and a complex isolation process, preventing sufficient material for in vivo efficacy and toxicity studies. Establishing a scalable synthetic route is therefore a critical, unmet prerequisite for advancing this bioactive natural product toward therapeutic candidacy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a convergent semi-synthetic strategy, utilizing a commercially available hydroquinone core and a stereoselective glycosylation, can efficiently produce multi-gram quantities of compound 10 with purity and stereochemical integrity suitable for preclinical development.
proposals.method: The route will begin with the chemical synthesis or commercial sourcing of the aglycone, 2-prenyl-1,4-hydroquinone. A key step will involve the development of a Koenigs-Knorr or Schmidt glycosylation protocol using a suitably protected derivative of D-glucose to stereoselectively install the β-glycosidic linkage. Protecting group strategy (e.g., acetyl, benzyl) will be optimized for high yield and facile deprotection. The final steps will involve selective prenyl group modification and global deprotection. All intermediates and the final product will be characterized by NMR, HRMS, and compared to the authentic natural sample.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first scalable access to a structurally unique anti-tau natural product, enabling comprehensive in vivo pharmacological evaluation in Alzheimer's disease models. It will establish a robust chemical platform for generating synthetic analogs to explore structure-activity relationships, potentially leading to more potent derivatives.
proposals.novelty: While the isolation and bioactivity of compound 10 were reported, no synthetic studies exist. This proposal moves beyond discovery to development, addressing the critical supply bottleneck through innovative synthetic chemistry tailored to this specific, complex glycoside, which is non-obvious given its sensitive hydroquinone and prenyl motifs.

proposals.title: Metabolic Stability and Degradation Pathway Profiling of Bioactive Prenylhydroquinone Glycosides and Methoxyflavones in Hepatic and Neuronal Cell Models
proposals.motivation: Despite the promising in vitro bioactivity of natural polyphenols, such as those isolated from Sedum kamtschaticum and Kaempferia parviflora, their therapeutic potential is often limited by poor metabolic stability and rapid degradation in biological systems. A systematic understanding of their specific metabolic fate is crucial for rational lead optimization but remains underexplored for many novel compound classes. This study addresses the critical gap between in vitro activity and in vivo efficacy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the prenylhydroquinone glycoside and methoxyflavones previously isolated by our group will exhibit distinct metabolic stability profiles in relevant cell models, and that their primary degradation pathways can be mapped to specific phase I and II enzymatic modifications.
proposals.method: We will employ LC-MS/MS-based metabolic stability assays in human liver microsomes (HLMs) and S9 fractions, as well as in SH-SY5Y and HepG2 cell lines, to calculate intrinsic clearance. Degradation products will be identified using high-resolution LC-QTOF-MS. Specific metabolic pathways (e.g., glucuronidation, sulfation, oxidation) will be probed using chemical inhibitors of key enzymes (CYP450s, UGTs, SULTs). Structural elucidation of major metabolites will be performed via NMR spectroscopy following scaled-up incubations and preparative HPLC.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive metabolic stability data and degradation pathway maps for these specific, recently isolated natural product classes. The findings will directly inform future structural modifications to enhance metabolic resistance and guide appropriate in vivo model selection for efficacy studies, bridging the gap between discovery and development.
proposals.novelty: While metabolic studies are common, a targeted, parallel investigation of the stability of these structurally distinct, newly reported anti-neurodegenerative and anti-melanogenic polyphenols in both hepatic and target neuronal/cancer cell models is novel. This dual-system approach reveals cell-type-specific metabolic vulnerabilities.

proposals.title: Metabolic Engineering of Plant Cell Cultures for Enhanced Production of a Bioactive Prenylhydroquinone Glycoside Targeting Tau Pathology
proposals.motivation: The prenylhydroquinone glycoside isolated from Sedum kamtschaticum shows promising activity against hyperphosphorylated tau, a key pathological marker in Alzheimer's disease, but its low natural abundance limits further pharmacological study and development. Plant cell culture technology offers a sustainable and scalable platform for producing valuable phytochemicals, yet metabolic engineering strategies for complex, bioactive polyphenols like this compound remain underdeveloped. Establishing a robust production system is critical for securing sufficient material for in-depth bioactivity and preclinical evaluation.
proposals.hypothesis: Targeted overexpression of key biosynthetic genes, combined with precursor feeding and elicitation in a dedifferentiated Sedum kamtschaticum cell culture, will significantly increase the yield of the target prenylhydroquinone glycoside. The engineered culture system will produce the compound with retained bioactivity against tau hyperphosphorylation in a neuronal cell model.
proposals.method: First, the biosynthetic pathway for the prenylhydroquinone glycoside will be partially elucidated via transcriptomic analysis of high- vs. low-producing plant tissues to identify candidate genes (e.g., prenyltransferases, glycosyltransferases, and polyphenol oxidases). Key candidate genes will be cloned and heterologously overexpressed in stable S. kamtschaticum callus cultures using Agrobacterium-mediated transformation. Cultures will be optimized with jasmonic acid elicitation and fed with predicted precursors (e.g., hydroquinone, isopentenyl diphosphate). Compound yield will be quantified using HPLC-DAD and LC-MS, and bioactivity will be validated via its effect on tau phosphorylation in Aβ1–42-treated SH-SY5Y cells.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first engineered plant cell culture platform for a therapeutically relevant prenylhydroquinone glycoside, providing a scalable source for neurodegenerative disease research. It will advance the metabolic engineering toolbox for polyphenol production in plant cultures and contribute functional genomic insights into the biosynthesis of this compound class. The project directly bridges natural product discovery and biotechnological application.
proposals.novelty: While metabolic engineering for flavonoids is common, applying this approach to enhance the yield of a recently discovered, structurally unique prenylhydroquinone glycoside with anti-tau activity is unprecedented. The strategy integrates pathway elucidation and cell culture engineering specifically for a compound with demonstrated neuroprotective potential, moving beyond simple compound isolation.

proposals.title: Decoupling Dual Cell Cycle Arrest: Investigating the Sufficiency of S-Phase or G2/M-Phase Blockade for the Antiproliferative Activity of Natural Prenylhydroquinone Glycosides
proposals.motivation: Many bioactive natural compounds, like the prenylhydroquinone glycoside recently isolated by our group from Sedum kamtschaticum, induce concurrent arrest in both S and G2/M phases of the cell cycle. This dual arrest is commonly reported but mechanistically underexplored; it remains unclear whether it represents a necessary synergistic mechanism or a secondary cascade effect. Determining the primary, causative arrest point is crucial for understanding the specific molecular targets of novel chemotypes and for designing more precise anti-proliferative agents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the observed dual S and G2/M phase arrest induced by specific natural compounds is not mechanistically obligatory, and that targeted pharmacological blockade of one key phase (either S or G2/M) will be sufficient to recapitulate the full antiproliferative effect.
proposals.method: We will employ synchronized SH-SY5Y and B16F10 cell cultures treated with our lead compound, the prenylhydroquinone glycoside from S. kamtschaticum. Using specific chemical inhibitors (e.g., aphidicolin for S-phase, RO-3306 for G2/M) and RNA interference (siRNA against key cyclins/CDKs), we will selectively block progression at either S or G2/M phase prior to compound treatment. Cell cycle distribution will be analyzed via flow cytometry with propidium iodide staining, and antiproliferative efficacy will be quantified via MTT and clonogenic assays. Western blotting for phosphorylated histone H3 (Ser10) and cyclin B1 will confirm G2/M arrest, while EdU incorporation will monitor S-phase progression.
proposals.contribution: This work will clarify the primary cell cycle checkpoint targeted by a novel class of natural prenylhydroquinone glycosides, moving beyond correlative observation to causative mechanism. It will establish a refined methodological framework for dissecting complex cell cycle effects of natural products. The findings could redefine structure-activity relationships by linking specific chemical motifs to discrete cell cycle interventions.
proposals.novelty: While dual-phase arrest is frequently reported, systematically testing its necessity by experimentally decoupling the phases for a specific natural chemotype is novel. This approach challenges the implicit assumption that concurrent arrests are integral to the compound's mechanism, rather than a downstream consequence.

proposals.title: A Source-Aware Computational Pipeline for the Targeted Isolation of Anti-Neurodegenerative Prenylated Phenolics from Sedum Species
proposals.motivation: Traditional bioactivity-guided isolation is resource-intensive and often leads to the re-isolation of known compounds. While computational models can predict bioactive scaffolds, a significant gap exists between in silico predictions and the actual presence of these compounds in a natural source. This proposal addresses the need for a more efficient and predictive discovery pipeline that minimizes wasted effort on compounds unlikely to exist or contribute to the observed bioactivity.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating computational prediction of bioactive scaffolds with source-specific metabolomic validation and microscale activity correlation will significantly increase the success rate and efficiency of isolating novel, target-active natural products.
proposals.method: We will develop a pipeline using: 1) A graph neural network (GNN) trained on pre-2025 NPASS and ChEMBL data to predict anti-tau aggregation scaffolds, focusing on prenylated phenolics. 2) LC-MS/MS-based untargeted metabolomics of Sedum kamtschaticum extracts to create a source-specific spectral library, with predicted structures screened via in silico fragmentation (e.g., using SIRIUS/CSI:FingerID) for plausible presence. 3) Microfluidic droplet-based assays to test crude fractions for tau aggregation inhibition, correlating activity peaks with MS features of the matched predictions. Isolation will proceed only for high-confidence, activity-linked targets.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, integrated methodology for natural product discovery that directly links prediction, source plausibility, and biological function. It is expected to yield new anti-neurodegenerative lead compounds from Sedum and establish a generalizable, closed-loop framework that reduces the empirical burden of traditional phytochemistry.
