proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its adversarial validation gate, which uses source metabolomics to filter computational predictions *before* isolation, and its use of microscale assays to functionally link predicted compounds to the source's bioactivity profile, moving beyond mere spectral matching.

proposals.title: Computationally Guided Refactoring of the Macrotermycin Pathway for the Biosynthesis of Novel Anti-Tau Flavonoid-Polyketide Hybrids
proposals.motivation: Natural product discovery is bottlenecked by the low-yield isolation of rare compounds from complex extracts and the difficulty of accessing structural analogs. Pre-2026, computational models for predicting bioactive scaffolds have advanced, but their validation is hindered by synthetic accessibility. Concurrently, the biosynthetic gene cluster (BGC) for macrotermycin, a structurally complex polyketide with a benzoate moiety, offers a versatile, engineerable template for producing flavonoid-like aromatic polyketides in Streptomyces.
proposals.hypothesis: That a refactored macrotermycin biosynthetic pathway, guided by computational predictions of anti-tau aggregation activity, can be reprogrammed to biosynthesize novel flavonoid-polyketide hybrid scaffolds with enhanced neuroprotective properties against Alzheimer's disease-related tau pathology.
proposals.method: First, we will employ a graph neural network (GNN)-based generative model, trained on pre-2026 databases like ChEMBL and NPASS, to design novel flavonoid-polyketide hybrid scaffolds predicted to inhibit hyperphosphorylated tau aggregation. Second, using sequence-structure modeling tools (e.g., AlphaFold2, pre-2026 version), we will identify key substrate specificity residues in the macrotermycin pathway's acyltransferase and ketosynthase domains. Third, we will refactor the entire BGC in a Streptomyces coelicolor host and perform site-saturation mutagenesis on the identified residues, screening for production of the computationally prioritized targets via LC-MS/MS.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm that directly links in silico molecular design with biosynthetic pathway engineering for on-demand natural product analog production. It will deliver a suite of novel, genetically encoded anti-tau compounds and a generalizable, refactored Streptomyces platform for flavonoid-polyketide biosynthesis, moving beyond traditional plant extraction methods.
proposals.novelty: The approach inverts the discovery pipeline by using computational prediction to drive pathway refactoring, rather than using genomics to find pathways for known structures. It uniquely repurposes the macrotermycin pathway, typically studied for its insecticidal properties, as a chassis for targeted neuroactive compound production.

proposals.title: Network Pharmacology-Guided Deconvolution of Complex Natural Extracts for Synergistic Anti-Neuroinflammatory Polypharmacology
proposals.motivation: Traditional natural product research often isolates single compounds, potentially losing the synergistic or polypharmacological benefits inherent in complex extracts, which may be crucial for treating multifactorial diseases like Alzheimer's. Pre-2026, network pharmacology and computational approaches have emerged to model multi-target effects, but their application to guide the fractionation and analysis of complex botanical matrices remains underexplored. This gap limits the systematic discovery of multi-component therapeutic agents from natural sources.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying a network pharmacology-based bioactivity prediction model to sequential fractions of a complex natural extract will identify specific sub-fractions and compound clusters whose combined multi-target profiles correlate with enhanced synergistic efficacy against neuroinflammation, surpassing the activity of isolated major constituents.
proposals.method: We will select a botanically rich source (e.g., Populus or Sedum species) and prepare a crude extract. Using LC-MS/MS-based untargeted metabolomics and GNPS molecular networking, we will map the chemical complexity. Sequential bioassay-guided fractionation against LPS-induced NO production in BV-2 microglial cells will be performed in parallel with in silico target prediction (using tools like SEA, SwissTargetPrediction) for major molecular families. A correlation network integrating chemical similarity clusters, predicted protein targets (e.g., COX-2, iNOS, TNF-α), and experimental bioactivity will be constructed to prioritize synergistic sub-fractions for detailed isolation (e.g., of prenylated phenolics or methoxyflavones) and validation in a multi-target assay panel.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological framework that retains and leverages chemical complexity as an informational asset for drug discovery. It is expected to yield new, potentially synergistic, multi-target bioactive natural compound clusters against neuroinflammation. The study will demonstrate how computational network analysis can directly guide experimental natural product chemistry towards polypharmacological outcomes.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard bioassay-guided isolation targeting a single active principle, this approach uses an integrated network model of chemical and predicted pharmacological space to deliberately track and preserve beneficial polypharmacology through the fractionation process, treating complexity as a design feature.

proposals.title: Network-Guided Cultivation and Standardization of Synergistic Multi-Component Fractions (SMCFs) from Microbial Extracts Targeting Tau Pathology
proposals.motivation: Traditional natural product drug discovery prioritizes the isolation of single, pure compounds, often overlooking the synergistic bioactivity inherent in complex metabolite mixtures. This is inefficient and discards potentially superior therapeutic entities. Pre-2026 advances in LC-MS/MS molecular networking (e.g., GNPS) now allow for the systematic visualization of entire biosynthetic families ('synergomes') in crude extracts, but this tool is primarily used to guide isolation of single analogs, not to define the multi-component mixture itself as the drug.
proposals.hypothesis: That specific clusters of co-produced microbial metabolites, identified via bioactivity-correlated molecular networking, can be deliberately cultivated and blended to form standardized, synergistic multi-component fractions (SMCFs) with enhanced and more consistent biological activity against neurodegenerative targets than any single constituent.
proposals.method: Microbial extracts (e.g., from actinomycetes) will be fractionated and screened for inhibition of hyperphosphorylated tau production in Aβ1–42-treated SH-SY5Y cells. Active fractions will be analyzed by LC-MS/MS, with data processed on the Global Natural Products Social Molecular Networking (GNPS) platform to construct molecular families. Network topology (cluster size, connectivity) and MS2 spectral similarity will be correlated with bioactivity to pinpoint synergistic clusters. Target clusters will be produced via optimized fermentation, and their constituent metabolites will be blended into a defined SMCF, standardized using quantitative NMR and LC-MS.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, data-driven pipeline for developing standardized natural product-based therapies that retain critical synergistic interactions. It will deliver a new class of defined biological entity—the SMCF—and demonstrate its application against a therapeutically relevant target in Alzheimer's disease pathology. The methodology shifts the paradigm from 'isolate and test' to 'map, correlate, cultivate, and blend.'
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in using molecular networking not as a pre-isolation tool, but as an analytical framework to rationally design, produce, and standardize the complex mixture itself as the final therapeutic agent, moving beyond the pure compound dogma in natural product drug discovery.

proposals.title: A Closed-Loop AI-Directed Platform for the Accelerated Discovery of Anti-Neurodegenerative Compounds from Natural Extracts
proposals.motivation: The discovery of novel bioactive natural products remains a slow, resource-intensive process dominated by linear, bioactivity-guided fractionation. This inefficiency is a major bottleneck in fields like neurodegenerative disease research, where new chemical scaffolds are urgently needed. While machine learning has been applied to virtual screening of compound libraries, its integration into the *real-time experimental workflow* of natural product isolation is unexplored, representing a significant opportunity to revolutionize the field.
proposals.hypothesis: An AI platform that uses real-time bioassay feedback to iteratively update predictive models and direct chromatographic fractionation will significantly increase the efficiency of isolating novel bioactive compounds from complex plant matrices compared to traditional methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a platform integrating liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS), automated fractionation, and cell-based bioassays (e.g., measuring tau hyperphosphorylation in SH-SY5Y cells). A machine learning model (e.g., a graph neural network or random forest) will be pre-trained on pre-2026 datasets of plant species, phytochemicals (like prenylhydroquinones and methoxyflavones), and associated bioactivities. This model will analyze initial LC-MS and bioassay data from crude extracts and early fractions, then predict the chromatographic regions most likely to contain target bioactivity. These predictions will automatically adjust the subsequent fractionation strategy, creating a closed-loop system where each round of bioassay data refines the model's guidance.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, adaptive platform that minimizes labor and material waste on inactive fractions, dramatically accelerating the isolation pipeline. It will yield new anti-neurodegenerative lead compounds and, as a byproduct, generate rich datasets revealing complex structure-activity relationships within plant metabolomes. The platform establishes a new paradigm for intelligent, data-driven natural product research.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the closed-loop, real-time integration of AI prediction with physical fractionation and bioassay, moving beyond static virtual screening to create an adaptive, learning-driven experimental process. This transforms the AI from a passive planning tool into an active director of the laboratory workflow.

proposals.title: Integrated Lead Optimization of a Prenylhydroquinone Glycoside from Sedum kamtschaticum: Structure-Activity Relationship, ADMET Prediction, and In Vitro Validation for Alzheimer's Disease
proposals.motivation: Despite the rich history of natural products in drug discovery, their development often stalls after initial bioactivity reports due to a lack of systematic, modern lead optimization. Pre-2026, frameworks integrating computational ADMET prediction, focused SAR, and multi-target profiling at the lead optimization stage are not routinely applied to novel plant isolates, limiting their translational potential.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a contemporary drug-development pipeline to a recently isolated, bioactive natural product lead (e.g., the prenylhydroquinone glycoside from Sedum kamtschaticum) will generate optimized semi-synthetic analogs with improved drug-like properties and enhanced efficacy against molecular targets in neurodegenerative disease.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-step approach: 1) Use the pre-2026 software Schrodinger's QikProp and SwissADME to computationally profile the ADMET properties of the parent compound and guide synthetic modification. 2) Conduct semi-synthesis to generate a focused library of analogs (e.g., varying glycoside moiety, prenyl chain length) based on the SAR framework. 3) Evaluate analogs in parallel for primary activity (tau phosphorylation in SH-SY5Y cells), secondary targets (e.g., Aβ aggregation, anti-inflammatory activity in microglia), and early cytotoxicity. 4) Apply multi-parameter optimization (MPO) scoring to rank leads.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, stepwise protocol for advancing plant-derived hits to lead compounds, moving beyond simple isolation and initial screening. It will deliver new chemical entities with optimized profiles for Alzheimer's disease and demonstrate the tangible value of integrating computational and medicinal chemistry principles early in natural product research.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic application of a pre-clinical lead optimization framework, including computational ADMET-driven design and MPO scoring, specifically to a newly discovered natural product scaffold immediately following its initial isolation and bioactivity report, a practice not standard in phytochemistry research pre-2026.

proposals.title: Development of a Real-Time Bioactivity-Correlated LC-MS Platform for the Function-Guided Discovery of Anti-Neurodegenerative Compounds from Natural Extracts
proposals.motivation: The discovery of bioactive natural products remains bottlenecked by the slow, sequential processes of bioassay-guided fractionation and subsequent structure elucidation of inactive components. This is particularly acute in neurodegenerative disease research, where complex molecular targets like tau and Aβ require functional screening. Pre-2026, no integrated platform exists to provide simultaneous, real-time structural and functional data streams from a single chromatographic run, leading to significant inefficiency in identifying true active principles from complex plant matrices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that coupling high-resolution liquid chromatography with parallel, real-time microfluidic bioassays and mass spectrometry will enable the immediate correlation of chromatographic peaks with specific biological activities, thereby allowing for the selective isolation and identification of only the bioactive constituents from a complex natural extract.
proposals.method: The platform will integrate an HPLC system with a post-column flow splitter. One stream will be directed to a high-resolution Q-TOF mass spectrometer (e.g., Agilent 6545) for ESI-MS/MS data acquisition. The parallel stream will be directed to a custom microfluidic chip containing immobilized SH-SY5Y neuronal cells, pre-treated with Aβ1–42 to induce hyperphosphorylated tau, as a live biosensor. A fluorescence-based readout (e.g., for tau phosphorylation or cell viability) will be recorded in real-time and synchronized with the MS chromatogram using custom LabVIEW software, creating a bioactivity-correlated molecular feature map.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel analytical platform that dramatically accelerates the discovery pipeline for natural product-based neurotherapeutics. It will provide a validated methodology for real-time, function-guided deconvolution of complex mixtures, moving the field beyond passive profiling. The immediate application will be the identification of new anti-neurodegenerative leads from previously studied plants like Sedum kamtschaticum or Populus species.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the real-time, parallel acquisition and software-based synchronization of high-resolution structural data (MS/MS) and functional cellular response data from a live, disease-relevant biosensor system within a single analytical run, creating an active, decision-making platform for isolation.

proposals.title: Integration of LC-MS/MS Molecular Networking with Residual Dipolar Coupling-Enhanced NMR for the De Novo Identification of Bioactive Compounds in Crude Plant Extracts
proposals.motivation: The rapid identification of bioactive compounds from complex natural product mixtures remains a major bottleneck in drug discovery. Traditional bioassay-guided fractionation is time-consuming and risks losing minor active constituents. Pre-2026, while LC-MS/MS molecular networking and conventional NMR are powerful, they often lack the resolution to definitively identify novel structures directly from crude extracts without extensive purification.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that coupling untargeted LC-MS/MS-based molecular networking with advanced NMR spectroscopy using anisotropic media to measure residual dipolar couplings (RDCs) will enable the rapid de novo structural elucidation of bioactive compounds, including stereochemistry, directly from partially purified sub-fractions of crude extracts.
proposals.method: Crude extracts from selected plants (e.g., Populus or Sedum species) will be analyzed by high-resolution LC-MS/MS to construct molecular networks using the Global Natural Products Social Molecular Networking (GNPS) platform. Bioactive clusters identified via parallel microtiter plate assays will guide the isolation of enriched sub-fractions by centrifugal partition chromatography. These sub-fractions will be analyzed using NMR in polyacrylamide gel aligning media to acquire 1D-1H and 2D spectra (HSQC, HMBC) with RDC enhancements. Computational structure elucidation will be performed by combining MS/MS fragmentation trees with RDC data, using algorithms like SENECA and COCON for 3D structure generation and ranking.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated hybrid analytical-computational pipeline that significantly accelerates the dereplication and discovery of novel bioactive natural products. It will contribute new spectroscopic protocols for obtaining stereochemical information from mixtures, moving the field beyond mere molecular formula identification towards complete structural assignment at an earlier stage of the isolation process.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of RDC-enhanced NMR with MS/MS molecular networking for mixture analysis is non-obvious, as RDCs are predominantly used for pure compounds or proteins. Applying this to partially purified natural product fractions to solve stereochemistry de novo represents a significant methodological advance over existing fragment-based or database-dependent approaches.

proposals.title: CRISPR-Cas9 Genome-Wide Knockout Screening to Deconvolute the Molecular Targets of Bioactive Crude Extracts from Korean Medicinal Plants
proposals.motivation: Natural product discovery is bottlenecked by the difficulty of identifying the specific protein targets and mechanisms of action (MoA) for bioactive compounds within complex crude extracts. Pre-2026, target identification typically requires isolating pure compounds first, a slow and often unsuccessful process. Applying pooled CRISPR knockout screening directly to crude extracts could revolutionize this workflow by rapidly pinpointing genetic vulnerabilities and potential drug targets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that genome-wide CRISPR-Cas9 knockout screens in human cell lines will reveal distinct, sensitizing gene knockouts for different crude plant extracts, thereby directly implicating specific genetic pathways and protein targets in their bioactive mechanisms.
proposals.method: We will perform pooled genome-wide CRISPR-Cas9 knockout screens (using the Brunello or GeCKO v2 libraries) in SH-SY5Y or HeLa cells treated with sub-lethal concentrations of crude extracts from Populus × tomentiglandulosa and Sedum kamtschaticum, plants previously studied by Kim. Cells surviving treatment will be harvested, and guide RNA sequences will be amplified and deep-sequenced. MAGeCK or BAGEL2 algorithms will be used to statistically identify significantly depleted or enriched sgRNAs, revealing genes whose knockout confers resistance or sensitivity to each extract.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct functional genomics pipeline for natural product MoA discovery, bypassing initial compound isolation. It will generate testable hypotheses about the primary targets and synergistic actions of components within Korean medicinal plant extracts. The method will provide a powerful pre-fractionation guide for subsequent phytochemical isolation efforts focused on the most promising bioactivities.
proposals.novelty: While CRISPR screens are standard in oncology and genetics, their direct application to deconvolute the targets of unpurified natural extracts is novel. This approach integrates modern functional genomics with traditional natural product research in a way not previously reported in Kim's prior isolation-focused work.

proposals.title: Integrating Untargeted Metabolomics and Bioactivity-Guided Fractionation for the Accelerated Discovery of Anti-Neuroinflammatory Compounds from Natural Sources
proposals.motivation: Traditional bioactivity-guided isolation of natural products is often a slow, iterative process, requiring repeated fractionation and bioassays on partially characterized mixtures. This bottleneck delays the identification of novel bioactive scaffolds. Pre-2026 advances in LC-MS-based untargeted metabolomics offer powerful tools for comprehensive chemical profiling, but their systematic integration into early-stage fractionation workflows to predict bioactive constituents remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: By applying untargeted metabolomics to fraction libraries from bioactive plant extracts, the spectral features (MS1 and MS/MS) of individual fractions can be correlated with their bioactivity scores to prioritize molecular ions associated with the observed effect, thereby guiding targeted isolation.
proposals.method: We will prepare a library of semi-purified fractions from a selected plant extract (e.g., Populus sp.) with known anti-neuroinflammatory activity in Aβ-induced SH-SY5Y or microglial cell models. All fractions will be analyzed using UHPLC-QTOF-MS/MS for untargeted profiling. Bioactivity data (e.g., inhibition of TNF-α or NO production) will be collected for each fraction. Correlation analysis, such as multivariate statistical modeling (Orthogonal Partial Least Squares, OPLS) and bioactivity-weighted molecular networking using GNPS or MetGem software, will be employed to highlight MS features whose intensity patterns across the fraction set align with the bioactivity profile. These prioritized features will guide subsequent targeted isolation via HPLC.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a streamlined pipeline that reduces the number of required bioassays by using metabolomic correlations to pinpoint bioactive metabolites early in the fractionation process. It will contribute novel anti-neuroinflammatory natural products and demonstrate a generalizable data-driven strategy to accelerate natural product discovery, moving beyond purely bioassay-driven or chemistry-driven approaches.
proposals.novelty: While metabolomics is used for crude extract profiling, its direct application to correlate complex MS data from a fraction library with discrete bioactivity scores to guide isolation is a novel, hypothesis-generating approach. This moves beyond simple chemical fingerprinting to create a predictive link between spectral abundance and biological function within a fractionation series.

proposals.title: In Vivo Bioactivity-Guided Fractionation of Populus × tomentiglandulosa Extracts for the Discovery of Novel Anti-Neuroinflammatory Compounds
proposals.motivation: The discovery of bioactive natural products is predominantly driven by in vitro cell-based assays, which often fail to predict in vivo efficacy due to poor bioavailability, metabolism, and complex systemic interactions. This gap leads to high attrition rates in drug development from natural sources. Establishing a direct link between fractionation and in vivo activity is a critical, unmet need in natural product chemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: That using a whole-animal disease model to guide the fractionation of a complex plant extract will lead to the isolation of compounds with genuine in vivo efficacy, which would be missed by conventional cell-based bioassay-guided isolation.
proposals.method: We will employ a murine model of LPS-induced neuroinflammation. A crude ethanol extract of Populus × tomentiglandulosa bark will be sequentially fractionated via liquid-liquid partitioning and medium-pressure liquid chromatography (MPLC). Each fraction will be administered intraperitoneally to model mice, and in vivo anti-neuroinflammatory activity will be assessed by measuring pro-inflammatory cytokine levels (TNF-α, IL-6) in brain homogenates and via immunohistochemical analysis of microglial activation (Iba1). The most active fraction will be iteratively sub-fractionated, with each step guided by the in vivo readout, until pure active compounds are obtained. Their structures will be elucidated using NMR spectroscopy and HR-ESI-MS.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a robust methodological pipeline for in vivo-guided isolation, directly linking phytochemical separation to therapeutic efficacy in a complex organism. It will yield novel anti-neuroinflammatory lead compounds with validated in vivo activity from Populus × tomentiglandulosa, contributing new chemical entities for neurodegenerative disease research. The approach will demonstrate a paradigm shift in natural product discovery workflows.
proposals.novelty: While bioassay-guided fractionation is standard, using a whole-animal disease model as the primary bioassay for the isolation process is highly innovative and non-routine. This moves the selection pressure for compound isolation from a simplified cellular environment to the physiologically relevant context of a living organism with intact pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics.

proposals.title: Targeted Isolation of Natural Products Inhibiting Tau Hyperphosphorylation from Korean Medicinal Plants: A Dual-Activity-Guided Fractionation Strategy
proposals.motivation: While most natural product discovery focuses on identifying bioactive compounds that induce a desired effect, there is a significant unmet need for selective inhibitors of pathological processes, such as tau hyperphosphorylation in Alzheimer's disease. Pre-2026 research, including the researcher's own work on Sedum kamtschaticum, highlights the potential of plant metabolites to modulate such targets, but systematic searches for pure inhibitors remain underexplored. This project addresses the 'dual problem' by specifically hunting for natural products that suppress, rather than induce, a key neurotoxic activity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that selected Korean medicinal plants, particularly those used in traditional cognitive health contexts, contain specific secondary metabolites that can selectively inhibit Aβ-induced tau hyperphosphorylation in neuronal cells without exhibiting general cytotoxicity.
proposals.method: The approach employs a dual-activity-guided fractionation. Extracts from candidate plants (e.g., selected Sedum or Populus species) will be prepared and tested in Aβ1–42-treated SH-SY5Y cells for their ability to reduce hyperphosphorylated tau (p-tau) levels, using immunoblotting as in the researcher's prior work. Active extracts will be fractionated via a combination of silica gel, Sephadex LH-20, and preparative HPLC. All fractions will be screened in parallel for p-tau inhibition and general cell viability (MTT assay) to rapidly discard cytotoxic or inactive fractions. Active, non-toxic leads will undergo structure elucidation using NMR (1H, 13C, 2D) and HR-ESI-MS.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel methodological framework for inhibitor-focused natural product discovery. It is expected to yield one or more new or known natural product inhibitors of tau pathology, providing new chemical tools for neurodegenerative disease research and validating a targeted approach to finding bioactive suppressants.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly inverting the typical bioactivity-guided isolation paradigm to focus on suppression of a specific pathological marker, and in the dual-assay design that concurrently monitors inhibition and cytotoxicity to ensure selective bioactivity.

proposals.title: Comparative Bioactivity and Safety Assessment of Purified Abietic Acid versus Whole Pinus densiflora Resin Extract: A Study on Anti-Inflammatory and Antimicrobial Efficacy
proposals.motivation: Natural product research often debates the therapeutic superiority of single compounds versus complex extracts. Pre-2026, abietic acid, a major diterpenoid in pine resin, is known for anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activities, but its efficacy relative to the whole resin extract, which contains synergistic or antagonistic compounds, is poorly characterized. This direct comparison is crucial for rationalizing the development of standardized phytopharmaceuticals versus traditional preparations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that purified abietic acid will exhibit superior and more specific in vitro bioactivity (anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial) compared to the whole pine resin extract, but the whole extract may demonstrate a broader safety profile in cytotoxicity assays due to the buffering effects of other constituents.
proposals.method: Pinus densiflora resin will be collected and extracted with ethanol. Abietic acid will be isolated via silica gel column chromatography and preparative HPLC, with purity verified by NMR and LC-MS. The anti-inflammatory activity of both samples will be assessed by measuring their inhibition of nitric oxide and pro-inflammatory cytokine (TNF-α, IL-6) production in LPS-stimulated RAW 264.7 macrophages. Antimicrobial efficacy will be tested against Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli using broth microdilution for MIC/MBC determination. Safety will be evaluated via MTT assay on normal human keratinocytes (HaCaT) and hepatocytes (HepG2) to compare cytotoxicity profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide definitive, head-to-head experimental data on the pharmacological trade-offs between a purified natural compound and its source extract. It will contribute to the field of natural product chemistry by offering a methodological framework for such comparisons and inform decisions in herbal medicine standardization and drug discovery pipelines regarding the value of isolation versus complex mixture use.
proposals.novelty: While abietic acid's bioactivities are known, a systematic, multi-assay comparison of its purified form against its native botanical matrix, specifically evaluating both efficacy and safety in parallel, is novel and addresses a fundamental, unresolved question in natural product research.

proposals.title: Systematic Isolation of Diterpenoids from Pinus densiflora Resin and Evaluation of Their Angiogenic and Wound-Healing Activities
proposals.motivation: Pine resin is a rich source of bioactive diterpenoids, such as dehydroabietic acid, known for their anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. However, a comprehensive phytochemical and pharmacological screening of the non-acidic diterpene fraction for specific pro-regenerative activities like angiogenesis remains underexplored. Identifying novel natural compounds that promote angiogenesis is crucial for developing new therapeutic agents for chronic wounds and impaired tissue repair.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pine resin contains structurally diverse diterpenoids beyond the well-characterized resin acids, and that specific compounds within this chemical space will exhibit significant pro-angiogenic and wound-healing activities in vitro.
proposals.method: Dried Pinus densiflora resin will be sequentially extracted and subjected to silica gel column chromatography, followed by semi-preparative HPLC to isolate individual diterpenoids. Structures will be elucidated using spectroscopic techniques (NMR, MS, IR). The isolated compounds will be screened for angiogenic potential using a human umbilical vein endothelial cell (HUVEC) tube formation assay on Matrigel and for wound-healing activity via a scratch assay on human dermal fibroblasts. Key angiogenic markers (e.g., VEGF, HIF-1α) will be quantified via qRT-PCR and ELISA.
proposals.contribution: This work will expand the known chemical inventory of Pinus densiflora and establish structure-activity relationships for pine diterpenoids in angiogenesis. It will identify specific lead compounds with therapeutic potential for wound healing, providing a natural product foundation for future drug development.
proposals.novelty: While anti-inflammatory activities of pine diterpenes are documented, a targeted, systematic isolation and screening campaign focused explicitly on pro-angiogenic diterpenoids from the understudied non-acidic fractions represents a novel approach to discovering wound-healing agents.

proposals.title: Elucidating the Pro-Angiogenic Signaling Pathways of Abietic Acid in Human Umbilical Vein Endothelial Cells (HUVECs): Focus on VEGF Receptor 2 and Downstream MAPK/ERK Activation
proposals.motivation: Angiogenesis, the formation of new blood vessels, is a critical therapeutic target for wound healing and ischemic diseases. While natural diterpenoids like abietic acid, isolated from plant resins such as pine, have shown preliminary bioactive potential, their precise molecular mechanisms in endothelial cells remain poorly characterized. A detailed, pathway-specific understanding is required to assess its therapeutic viability and avoid off-target effects associated with broad VEGF pathway modulation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that abietic acid stimulates angiogenesis in HUVECs primarily by activating VEGF Receptor 2 (VEGFR2) tyrosine kinase, leading to the phosphorylation and activation of the downstream MAPK/ERK signaling cascade, thereby promoting cell proliferation and tube formation.
proposals.method: We will treat HUVECs with purified abietic acid and assess angiogenesis using a Matrigel tube formation assay and a cell proliferation (MTT) assay. Key signaling events will be analyzed via western blotting to measure phosphorylation levels of VEGFR2, ERK1/2, and other relevant nodes (e.g., Akt, p38 MAPK). Specific pharmacological inhibitors (e.g., SU5416 for VEGFR2, U0126 for MEK/ERK) will be employed to confirm the necessity of each pathway. All experiments will include VEGF-positive and vehicle-negative controls, with data analyzed using one-way ANOVA followed by Tukey's post-hoc test.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first detailed mechanistic map of abietic acid's pro-angiogenic action, directly linking its activity to specific receptor activation and intracellular signaling. It will establish a foundational pharmacological profile for abietic acid as a potential lead compound for angiogenesis-related therapies and contribute to the broader understanding of diterpenoid bioactivity in vascular biology.
proposals.novelty: While abietic acid's isolation and general bioactivity are known, its specific agonist effect on VEGFR2 and downstream MAPK/ERK signaling in endothelial cells is unreported. This proposal moves beyond simple activity screening to a targeted, pathway-driven elucidation, aligning with the researcher's expertise in isolating and characterizing natural compounds with specific cellular effects.

proposals.title: Deconstructing the Role of Glycosylation in a Prenylhydroquinone Antifungal Agent: Targeted Synthesis, Enzymatic Reconstitution, and Functional Evaluation
proposals.motivation: Many antifungal natural products are isolated as glycosides, and the sugar moiety is often assumed to be essential for bioactivity, though its precise role is rarely dissected. This assumption limits structure-activity relationship (SAR) understanding and potential optimization. A systematic, chemical biology approach is needed to determine if the sugar is part of the pharmacophore, a targeting handle, or a modulator of physicochemical properties.
proposals.hypothesis: The glycosyl moiety of a specific prenylhydroquinone antifungal agent is not part of the core pharmacophore but serves as a critical modulator of solubility and cellular uptake, and its structure can be altered without complete loss of activity.
proposals.method: Using a recently isolated antifungal prenylhydroquinone glycoside as a model, we will first chemically hydrolyze it to produce the core aglycone. We will then employ characterized glycosyltransferases (e.g., from the UGT family) to reconstitute the native glycoside in vitro. Subsequently, we will use enzymatic synthesis with sugar-nucleotide donors (UDP-glucose, UDP-deoxyglucose) or chemical glycosylation to generate a series of sugar variants. The antifungal activity of the aglycone, the reconstituted native glycoside, and the variant glycosides will be rigorously compared against Candida and Aspergillus species using standardized microdilution assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a clear functional map of the glycosyl 'handle' in a specific antifungal scaffold, moving beyond correlative observations to mechanistic insight. It will establish a generalizable chemical biology workflow for probing glycosylation roles in other bioactive natural products. The findings could guide the design of simplified, more potent analogs by identifying dispensable or modifiable sugar units.
proposals.novelty: While enzymatic glycosylation is known, its application to systematically 'rebuild' and vary the sugar on a known antifungal aglycone for definitive functional dissection is novel. This approach directly challenges the common but untested assumption of essential glycosylation in natural product bioactivity.

proposals.title: Glycosylation-Dependent Antifungal Activity in Natural Products from Non-Plant Extremophiles: A Systematic Source-to-SAR Study
proposals.motivation: The rise of drug-resistant fungal pathogens necessitates novel antifungal scaffolds. Pre-2026, natural product discovery has heavily relied on plants and soil microbes, but extremophiles from high-temperature, high-salinity, or high-pressure niches remain underexplored as sources of antifungals. These organisms likely produce unique glycosylated compounds where sugar moieties may be crucial for bioactivity or stability in their native harsh conditions, a hypothesis rarely tested directly upon discovery.
proposals.hypothesis: Extremophile-derived antifungal natural products possess unique, essential glycosylation patterns critical for their biological activity, which are not redundant and differ fundamentally from patterns observed in plant-derived molecules.
proposals.method: We will systematically isolate antifungal compounds from cultured thermophilic bacteria (e.g., *Thermus* spp.), halophilic archaea, and deep-sea fungi using bioassay-guided fractionation (against *Candida albicans* and *Aspergillus fumigatus*). For each active glycoside identified via NMR and MS, its aglycone will be generated in parallel via enzymatic deglycosylation or chemical synthesis. The antifungal activity, stability under thermal and osmotic stress, and membrane interaction (via fluorescence anisotropy assays) of each native glycoside and its aglycone will be compared directly to determine glycosylation's role.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver new antifungal lead compounds from novel extremophile sources. More importantly, it will generate a unique dataset correlating specific glycosylation patterns with bioactivity and stability in extreme-environment microbes, providing foundational rules for glycosylation essentiality in natural product chemistry. This moves beyond mere discovery to immediate mechanistic insight.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of extremophile-focused bioprospecting with immediate, parallel generation of aglycones for every hit creates a systematic test of glycosylation essentiality in a context (extreme environments) where it is hypothesized to be paramount, a direct approach not commonly applied in natural product research pre-2026.

proposals.title: Structure-Activity Relationship Study of Prenylhydroquinone Glycosides: Enzymatic and Chemical Deglycosylation to Probe the Role of Sugar Moieties in Antifungal Activity
proposals.motivation: Natural product glycosides are a major source of bioactive leads, but the specific contribution of the sugar moiety to their biological activity is often unclear. In the pre-2026 context of rising antifungal resistance and the need for new antifungal scaffolds, understanding the pharmacophore of promising natural compounds is crucial for rational drug design and semi-synthetic optimization. This work directly addresses a fundamental gap in the SAR of recently discovered prenylhydroquinone glycosides.
proposals.hypothesis: The glycosyl moiety of the prenylhydroquinone glycoside isolated from Sedum kamtschaticum is essential for its observed antifungal activity, and its removal (aglycone generation) will result in a significant reduction or complete loss of activity.
proposals.method: The target prenylhydroquinone glycoside will be isolated from S. kamtschaticum leaves using established chromatographic techniques (e.g., VLC, MPLC, prep-HPLC). The aglycone will be generated via enzymatic hydrolysis using β-glucosidase and via mild acid hydrolysis. All compounds (native glycoside, enzymatically-derived aglycone, and acid-derived aglycone) will be purified and structurally confirmed by NMR and HRMS. Their antifungal activity will be evaluated in parallel against a panel of clinically relevant fungal strains (e.g., Candida albicans, Cryptococcus neoformans) using standardized broth microdilution assays to determine Minimum Inhibitory Concentrations (MICs).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide definitive experimental evidence on the role of glycosylation in the bioactivity of a specific class of natural products. It will establish a clear structure-activity relationship for these prenylhydroquinone derivatives, guiding future isolation strategies and medicinal chemistry efforts. The results will be directly applicable to the optimization of natural product-based antifungal agents.
proposals.novelty: While Kim's group has expertly isolated and reported the novel glycoside, its specific SAR regarding the sugar unit is unexplored. Systematically producing and testing the aglycone against the native compound to decouple glycosylation from core scaffold effects is a logical but previously unreported step for this specific natural product, moving beyond mere discovery to mechanistic understanding.

proposals.title: Phenotypic Network Profiling of Macrotermycins for Non-Antibiotic Immunomodulatory and Signaling Activities in Mammalian Cells
proposals.motivation: The discovery of novel bioactive compounds from natural sources has been largely siloed, with antimicrobials rarely screened for functions in mammalian signaling systems. This represents a missed opportunity, as many natural products possess pleiotropic bioactivities. Inspired by allelopathy research where plant defense compounds are evaluated for broader signaling roles, a systematic, multi-parametric phenotypic screen of known microbial metabolites could reveal new therapeutic leads for immune and inflammatory disorders.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that macrotermycins, a class of bacterial-derived antifungal compounds, possess latent, non-antimicrobial bioactivities that modulate specific phenotypic networks in mammalian immune and stromal cells.
proposals.method: We will apply purified macrotermycins (A, B, C, etc.) to a panel of human cell lines, including THP-1 macrophages, Jurkat T cells, and primary human dermal fibroblasts. A multi-tiered phenotypic assay will be employed, using high-content imaging to quantify cell morphology and migration, flow cytometry to measure surface marker expression (e.g., CD80, CD86, MHC-II), and multiplex ELISA/Luminex to profile secreted cytokine/chemokine levels. Metabolic activity will be assessed via Seahorse analyzer and MTT assay. Data integration will use principal component analysis and network correlation mapping to identify compound-specific phenotypic signatures.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel bioactivity profile for the macrotermycin class beyond its known antifungal role, potentially identifying lead compounds for immunomodulation or tissue repair. It will provide a methodological blueprint for the systematic phenotypic repurposing of antimicrobial natural products, bridging microbial ecology and mammalian cell pharmacology.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct analogical translation of a multi-tiered screening strategy from plant allelopathy research to the functional characterization of microbial metabolites in mammalian systems, moving beyond target-based screens to uncover network-level phenotypic effects.

ideas.title: Probing the Glycosyl 'Handle' via Targeted Chemical Synthesis and Functional Reconstitution
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from pharmacology and chemical biology, where the role of specific chemical groups in drugs is deconstructed via synthesis and reconstitution. The analogous problem is determining if a functional group on a bioactive molecule is part of the active pharmacophore or a targeting/prodrug handle. The solution involves producing the core aglycone and then systematically rebuilding the glycosyl moiety with variations. Adapted to this glycosylated antifungal context, the research would involve: 1) producing the aglycone, 2) enzymatically/chemically re-glycosylating it with the native sugar to confirm activity restoration, and 3) synthesizing or enzymatically generating variants with altered sugar structures (e.g., glucose instead of rhamnose, or a deoxy sugar). By comparing the antifungal activity of this series, the assay can distinguish whether the sugar is essential as a specific binding element, a solubility modulator, or entirely dispensable, thereby rigorously challenging the assumption that the native glycosylation is essential for bioactivity.

ideas.title: Integrated Real-Time Bioactivity-Correlated Structure Elucidation Platform for Complex Mixtures
ideas.description: This research develops an integrated analytical platform that directly couples high-resolution chromatographic separation with parallel, real-time biological activity monitoring and structural characterization. The system uses a post-column flow splitter to direct fractions simultaneously to a high-resolution mass spectrometer for structural data and to microfluidic bioassay cells containing immobilized molecular targets or biosensor cells, generating instant bioactivity profiles. This direct correlation allows for the immediate identification of only those chromatographic features associated with a desired biological effect, dramatically accelerating the discovery process. The approach moves beyond passive mixture analysis to active, function-guided isolation of lead compounds from complex natural extracts.

This systematic approach will test the hypothesis that extremophile-derived antifungals may possess unique glycosylation patterns essential for activity in their native harsh environments, patterns not seen in plant-derived molecules. By fusing source-focused discovery with immediate structure-activity relationship probing, the work aims to reveal if extreme environments select for bioactive compounds where sugar moieties play a non-redundant, critical role in target interaction or stability.

ideas.title: Predictive Isolation with Source-Aware and Microscale Activity Validation
ideas.description: This approach inverts traditional fractionation by starting with computational prediction of bioactive scaffolds. However, it directly addresses the prediction-reality gap by implementing a critical validation gate before committing to isolation. Predicted structures are first screened against metabolomic or genomic data from the candidate natural source to confirm their plausible biosynthetic presence. For the most promising, matched predictions, the specific crude extract or early fraction is then tested in a microfluidic or high-throughput assay tailored to the predicted biological target. This creates a closed loop where isolation efforts are only launched for computationally predicted compounds that are both likely to exist in the source and demonstrably contribute to the source's bioactivity at a microscale.

In allelopathy research, compounds initially studied for growth inhibition are screened for broader signaling roles using multi-tiered phenotypic assays on model plants. Analogously, we propose to screen macrotermycins for non-antimicrobial functions by applying them to a panel of mammalian immune and stromal cells and measuring a comprehensive set of phenotypic responses (e.g., cytokine secretion, surface marker changes, metabolic activity, migration). This systematic phenotypic network assay will identify whether these compounds modulate specific immune or cellular signaling pathways, revealing potential applications in immunomodulation or tissue signaling contexts.

ideas.title: Computationally Guided Biosynthetic Pathway Refactoring for Targeted Natural Product Discovery

Instead of attempting to isolate these predicted molecules from complex natural extracts, the project uses the elucidated macrotermycin biosynthetic pathway as a template. The core synergy is the refactoring and engineering of this pathway, guided by the computational predictions, to biosynthesize the target structures in a tractable host like Streptomyces. This approach bypasses the low-yield isolation bottleneck and directly tests the bioactivity of computationally designed molecules via genetically programmed production.

ideas.description: This research develops an integrated platform where machine learning models, trained on ethnobotanical, phytochemical, and bioassay data, actively direct the experimental workflow of bioactivity-guided fractionation. The system uses initial bioassay results from early fractions to update its models in real-time, predicting which subsequent fractions or chromatographic conditions are most likely to contain novel bioactive compounds. This creates a closed-loop feedback system that dramatically increases the efficiency of isolation by minimizing wasted effort on inactive fractions and adaptively focusing resources on the most promising chemical spaces. The outcome is a continuously learning platform that accelerates discovery and uncovers complex structure-activity relationships hidden within plant extracts.

ideas.title: Network-Guided Development of Synergistic Multi-Component Fractions (SMCFs)
ideas.description: This research direction applies LC-MS/MS molecular networking to microbial extracts not for the purpose of isolating a single pure analog, but to rapidly visualize and characterize the entire co-produced chemical family, or 'synergome,' around a core scaffold like macrotermycin. By correlating network topology with bioactivity data from crude fractions, the approach identifies specific clusters of metabolites that contribute to a desired synergistic effect. These characterized, multi-component clusters are then deliberately cultivated, blended, and standardized as Synergistic Multi-Component Fractions (SMCFs), which become the defined therapeutic entity. This bypasses the inefficiency of pure compound isolation while adding a rigorous, data-driven framework to ensure consistency, biological relevance, and manufacturability of complex natural product-based therapies.

The core synergy lies in selecting source organisms not just for their chemical novelty, but also for their inherent genetic and physiological tractability in fermentation, using Amycolatopsis sp. M39 as a foundational model. This approach ensures that promising lead compounds are not stranded in discovery due to supply limitations, accelerating the entire pipeline from environmental sample to gram-scale compound production for downstream evaluation.

ideas.title: High-Throughput Single-Cell Functional Metabolomics of Termite Gut Symbionts
ideas.description: This research integrates single-termite microbiome profiling with an interdisciplinary pipeline for natural product discovery. Individual termite guts are dissociated, and their microbial cells are sorted via high-throughput microfluidics or FACS, followed by single-cell genomics and parallelized, miniaturized culturing or direct metabolite extraction. The unified pipeline then applies rapid LC-MS/MS-based metabolomics and structure elucidation directly to these single-symbiont samples, coupled with high-throughput bioassays. This approach directly correlates a producing microbe's genetic identity with its chemical product and bioactivity, transforming the search for macrotermycin-like compounds from a bulk environmental sample problem into a targeted, cell-by-cell functional analysis.

ideas.title: Adapting Drug Discovery's Virtual Screening Pipeline for Ethnobotanical Prioritization
ideas.description: This idea adapts the established virtual screening pipeline from computational drug discovery to the problem of plant selection for ethnobotanical research. In drug discovery, ML models are trained on chemical structure and bioactivity data to prioritize molecules from vast virtual libraries for synthesis and testing. The analogous solution is adapted by constructing a multi-modal dataset for plants, combining features from ethnobotanical use records, known phytochemical profiles (as molecular fingerprints or compound classes), phylogenetic data, and ecological/geographical traits. Machine learning models (e.g., graph neural networks for phytochemical structures, ensemble methods for mixed data) would be trained to predict a composite 'bioactivity promise' score. This system would function as a virtual screening tool for biological sources, outputting a ranked list of candidate species for field collection and phytochemical analysis, thereby optimizing the resource-intensive discovery pipeline for novel plant-derived compounds.

ideas.title: Adapting Astronomical Signal Deconvolution for Spectral Analysis of Natural Extracts
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to develop advanced spectroscopic/computational tools for rapid identification of active compounds in crude natural extracts by drawing an analogy from astronomy. In astronomy, algorithms like CLEAN deconvolve blended signals from multiple celestial sources within a single data stream. The adapted idea is to treat the complex, overlapping spectroscopic data (e.g., from NMR, LC-MS, or IR) of a natural extract as an astronomical signal containing blended 'sources' (individual compounds). By applying and refining similar iterative deconvolution and compressed sensing algorithms, informed by comprehensive spectral libraries and mixture-specific noise models, the tool would computationally isolate the pure spectral signature of each constituent. This would dramatically accelerate the identification of bioactive compounds directly from complex mixtures without the need for complete prior physical separation.

ideas.title: Systematic Lead Optimization of Co-Evolved Antifungal Countermeasures
ideas.description: This research direction isolates fungi from the same ecological niche that are resistant to macrotermycins, but instead of solely studying co-evolution, it treats the fungi's resistance mechanisms (e.g., efflux pumps, target site mutations, detoxifying enzymes) as a source of 'validated' molecular counter-strategies. Modern drug-development principles—such as structure-activity relationship (SAR) analysis, medicinal chemistry optimization, and in vitro ADMET profiling—are then systematically applied to the natural products or molecular targets involved in this resistance. This approach directly leverages billions of years of fungal evolutionary pressure to identify and optimize lead compounds with a higher probability of overcoming common resistance pathways, creating a novel pipeline for next-generation antifungals.

ideas.title: Treating Natural Extract Complexity as a High-Dimensional Signal for Network Pharmacology
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that the chemical complexity of natural extracts is a barrier to drug discovery by treating it as an advantage, analogous to how astrophysicists treat complex cosmic signals. In astrophysics, techniques like interferometry and statistical decomposition parse complex, noisy data to identify multiple distinct sources. Adapted to pharmacology, we propose to treat the intricate chemical mixture of a natural extract not as 'noise' to be purified away, but as a high-dimensional signal encoding a network pharmacology profile. By applying multivariate statistical and machine learning models (e.g., bioactivity-guided PCA or network analysis), the collective bioactivity can be deconvoluted to map synergistic clusters of compounds onto specific biological pathways or polypharmacological outcomes, turning complexity into a direct guide for discovering multi-target drug combinations or synergistic phytotherapeutic profiles.

ideas.title: High-Throughput Microfluidic Bioactivity Screening for Parallelized Fractionation
ideas.description: This idea adapts high-throughput screening solutions from proteomics and drug discovery to streamline bioactivity-guided isolation of natural products. The analogous field uses miniaturized, automated microfluidic and droplet-based platforms to screen thousands of compounds in parallel with minimal reagent use. The adapted research idea involves creating microfluidic chips that perform parallel nanoliter-scale fractionation of plant extracts coupled with integrated on-chip bioassays. This system would simultaneously test hundreds of micro-fractions for activity, automatically routing only the active ones for further analysis, thereby creating a more efficient, rapid, and resource-conserving fractionation method.

ideas.title: Closed-Loop AI-Driven Natural Product Optimization Platform
ideas.description: This research direction integrates machine learning for plant prioritization directly into the iterative cycle of modern drug development for natural products. Machine learning models analyze ethnobotanical, ecological, and chemical data to predict not just promising source organisms, but also specific chemical scaffolds with high optimization potential. These prioritized leads then enter a structured optimization pipeline employing principles like structure-activity relationship (SAR) analysis, pharmacokinetic modeling, and synthetic derivatization. Crucially, all experimental data generated from this optimization—including success and failure metrics—feeds back into the ML models, creating a closed-loop system that learns from the drug-development process itself to become increasingly precise at predicting viable natural product drug candidates from the outset.

ideas.title: Synergistic Metabolite Detection via Noise-Suppression and Co-cultivation
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that bioactivity is solely from macrotermycins by adapting a solution from astrophysics. In exoplanet detection, the bright light of a star is blocked to reveal faint orbiting planets. Analogously, we propose to chemically or genetically suppress the biosynthesis of the dominant macrotermycins in the producing organism, thereby reducing their overwhelming signal in bioassays and analytical chemistry profiles. This 'noise suppression' will allow the detection of other, previously masked, bioactive compounds. Furthermore, inspired by methods to infer hidden planets through stellar wobble, we will use microbial co-cultivation to induce the expression of silent biosynthetic pathways, potentially revealing novel active metabolites that contribute to the observed bioactivity.

ideas.title: Affinity-selection molecular networking for bioactivity-guided analog discovery
ideas.description: This redesigned system couples LC-MS/MS molecular networking with an immediate, integrated bioactivity filter to overcome the pure structure-based prioritization flaw. Crude extracts from related strains are first incubated with immobilized target protein (e.g., the bacterial membrane component targeted by macrotermycins), and only the bound ligands are eluted and analyzed by LC-MS/MS. The resulting molecular network is constructed exclusively from compounds demonstrating target affinity, ensuring all prioritized analogs have a confirmed mechanism-of-action link. This approach rapidly converges on functionally relevant analogs, dramatically reducing downstream validation efforts on inactive structural relatives.

ideas.title: Computational Target-Directed Mining of Natural Products
ideas.description: This idea inverts the traditional bioactivity-guided fractionation starting point by drawing an analogy from mineral exploration. In that field, the solution is to first use computational geological modeling to predict the location of valuable ore deposits before physical excavation. Adapted to natural product research, the method begins with computational prediction of bioactive structures against a specific target (e.g., a disease-related protein) from vast digital libraries of known natural products. Researchers then target the isolation of only these computationally prioritized 'hits' from their source organisms, transforming the process from a blind screening of complex mixtures to a targeted, hypothesis-driven mining operation for predicted bioactive compounds.

ideas.title: Screen macrotermycins for host-directed, pathogen-specific immunomodulation
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a dual-pathogen and host-cell screening strategy for macrotermycins against a broad panel of human and agricultural pathogens. The key innovation is to assay not only for direct antimicrobial killing but also for compound-induced changes in host immune signaling pathways during infection. The goal is to identify macrotermycin variants that act as 'immune-guided antimicrobials,' which may disarm a pathogen directly while also boosting specific, beneficial host defenses or suppressing detrimental inflammation unique to that pathogen-host interaction. This could lead to novel therapeutic strategies that are less prone to driving resistance and more effective against complex, persistent infections.

ideas.title: Standardized Synergistic Fractions: A Systems Pharmacology Approach
ideas.description: This research challenges the core pharmacological assumption that a therapy must be based on a single, isolated molecular entity. Drawing an analogy from complex materials science and alloy engineering, where desired material properties emerge from synergistic mixtures of elements rather than pure substances, we propose a 'systems pharmacology' approach. The analogous solution—specifying and manufacturing to a precise compositional and processing standard to guarantee consistent emergent properties—is adapted to develop therapies based on characterized, synergistic multi-component fractions. The research would focus on establishing frameworks for defining these 'pharmacological alloys,' creating robust manufacturing protocols to ensure batch-to-batch reproducibility of the fraction's biological activity profile, and developing new regulatory science standards that validate efficacy and safety based on the consistent performance of the characterized mixture rather than the chemical purity of a single compound.

ideas.title: Phenotype-first CRISPR screening with single-cell transcriptomics for natural product mechanism elucidation
ideas.description: This adversarially improved approach first subjects cells to crude natural extracts and uses high-content imaging to sort live cells based on specific, pre-defined cytological phenotypes indicative of a particular cellular process. This phenotype-enriched population is then processed for single-cell RNA sequencing to identify the immediate transcriptional responses and perturbed pathways. Finally, CRISPR knockout or activation screens are performed in a targeted manner on the genes and pathways highlighted by the scRNA-seq data to validate their necessity for the observed phenotype, thereby directly linking the extract's bioactivity to a specific, novel mechanism of action with high confidence.

ideas.title: Scale up to automated structure elucidation pipelines

ideas.title: AI-Guided Spectral Deconvolution and Analog Mapping for Complex Mixtures
ideas.description: This research direction develops a novel computational platform that integrates LC-MS/MS molecular networking with advanced machine learning for spectral deconvolution. The platform uses the connectivity within a molecular network (e.g., of macrotermycin analogs) as a topological constraint to guide the separation of overlapping spectroscopic signals from crude extracts. This allows for the simultaneous prediction of core structures and variable modifications for all network members directly from mixture data. The result is a paradigm shift from isolating and then characterizing single analogs to the direct, high-throughput mapping of entire biosynthetic families and their structure-activity relationships.

ideas.title: CRISPR-Guided Minimal Biosynthetic Pathway Reconstruction for Mechanism Discovery
ideas.description: This research direction first uses CRISPR-based knockout screening in the native macrotermycin producer to definitively identify the minimal gene set required for core scaffold biosynthesis. This minimal pathway is then heterologously expressed to generate a focused, structurally simplified 'core' compound library. Finally, these purified core scaffolds and their biosynthetic intermediates are used as precise molecular probes in parallel CRISPR knockout screens in human cells, enabling the unambiguous identification of their cellular targets and mechanisms of action, bypassing the complexity of crude extracts.

ideas.title: Mechanism-first screening of macrotermycins using integrated phenotypic and target-deconvolution platforms
ideas.description: This adversarially improved idea screens macrotermycins against a broad pathogen panel not just for growth inhibition, but with built-in, parallel assays to immediately elucidate mechanism of action. The primary screening platform uses resistant mutants, cytological profiling, and genomic/transcriptomic signatures to classify hits in real-time, filtering out compounds with non-specific or known mechanisms. This ensures that only hits with novel and specific targets against high-priority pathogens are prioritized for downstream development, dramatically increasing the likelihood of discovering a viable lead candidate.

ideas.title: ML-Guided Prioritization and Heterologous Expression of Cryptic Fungal Biosynthetic Pathways in Streptomyces
ideas.description: This research direction integrates a machine learning model trained on fungal genomic, ecological, and ethnobotanical data to predict which fungi harbor cryptic biosynthetic gene clusters (BGCs) with high potential for novel bioactive compound production. The key innovation is that the model is also trained on features relevant to successful heterologous expression in Streptomyces, such as promoter compatibility, codon usage, and predicted toxicity of pathway intermediates. The highest-scoring predicted BGCs are then directly cloned and expressed in a tailored Streptomyces host system. This synergy enables a systematic, high-throughput pipeline to access the hidden chemical diversity of fungi by intelligently prioritizing targets that are both genetically promising and technically feasible to express in a tractable production host.

ideas.title: Apply a 'Materials Genome Initiative' framework to natural product discovery
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from the Materials Genome Initiative (MGI) in Materials Science, which integrated computational design, high-throughput synthesis, and rapid characterization into a unified, data-driven pipeline. The adapted research idea applies this framework to natural product discovery by creating a closed-loop system. It starts with computational prediction of promising sources, moves to automated extraction and fractionation, then to integrated, high-throughput structure elucidation (e.g., microcryoprobe NMR coupled with LC-MS) and bioactivity screening. The key adaptation is using machine learning to analyze all generated data—genomic, metabolomic, and bioassay—to form a predictive model that actively guides the next cycle of source selection and hypothesis testing, dramatically accelerating the discovery pipeline.

ideas.title: Process-Locked, Profile-Defined Polypharmaceutical Therapeutics
ideas.description: This approach develops therapies from synergistic multi-component mixtures but overcomes the standardization flaw by making the exact, reproducible manufacturing process and the resulting analytical profile core parts of the therapeutic definition. The final product is defined not by a list of compounds, but by a locked-down production protocol and a verifiable multi-omics signature (e.g., metabolomic, proteomic) with acceptable tolerance ranges for key features. This 'process-profile' combination is then rigorously correlated to a consistent biological potency signature in validated assays, enabling regulatory approval of a complex but precisely characterized and reproducible biological entity.

ideas.title: Phylogeny-guided dissection of niche-specific vs. generalist antifungal resistance in fungi
ideas.description: This study isolates fungi from the same niche as a macrotermycin-producing strain and screens for resistance. To distinguish co-evolutionary resistance from generalist traits, it employs a comparative genomic analysis of resistant isolates against a broad phylogenetic panel of related fungi from distant, antibiotic-free niches. Resistance mechanisms unique to the shared niche cluster, particularly those involving the drug target, are identified as candidate co-evolutionary adaptations. This targeted approach ensures subsequent functional studies focus on resistance traits most likely resulting from the specific arms race with the macrotermycin producer.

ideas.title: Supply-Aware Lead Optimization for Natural Products via Integrated Biosynthesis Engineering
ideas.description: This approach systematically applies modern drug-development principles to natural products while simultaneously solving the critical supply chain flaw. It establishes a parallel track during lead optimization where biosynthetic pathway analysis and engineering strategies are developed concurrently with SAR studies. The framework uses computational tools to predict and prioritize synthetic routes (microbial or chemical) for optimized analogs, ensuring selected candidates are not just potent but also sustainably producible at scale. This creates a closed-loop design process where manufacturability constraints directly inform medicinal chemistry decisions, yielding robust drug candidates with viable development pathways.

ideas.title: Adapting Soil Nutrient Imaging to Map Antibiotic Distribution in Termite Nest Structures
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from soil science, where techniques like Diffusive Gradients in Thin-films (DGT) and Planar Optode imaging are used to create high-resolution 2D maps of nutrient availability in complex, heterogeneous environments like soil and rhizospheres. The analogous problem is visualizing a bioactive compound's distribution within a complex, porous, and architecturally varied biological matrix. The adapted solution is to apply Desorption Electrospray Ionization (DESI) mass spectrometry imaging to termite nest cross-sections. This technique, analogous to chemical mapping in soil, will provide in situ, label-free visualization of macrotermycin distribution and potential metabolites. The resulting chemical maps will reveal how the antibiotic permeates different nest compartments (e.g., nursery, fungus garden, gallery walls), offering insights into its role in disease prevention and the chemical ecology of the termite symbiosis.

ideas.title: Phylogenetic Frontier Bioprospecting: Targeting Evolutionarily Distinct Lineages for Novel Chemistry
ideas.description: This idea challenges the plant-centric assumption in natural product discovery by adapting a solution from microbial ecology. In antibiotic discovery, moving beyond common soil actinomycetes to phylogenetically rare bacterial phyla from extreme environments unlocked novel chemistry. We adapt this by making evolutionary distinctness, not just environmental source, the primary criterion for bioprospecting. The research program would systematically map the phylogenetic diversity of fungi, marine invertebrates, protists, and extremophiles, identifying the most evolutionarily distant and understudied lineages. Targeted sampling, specialized cultivation (e.g., using microfluidics or host-mimicking media), and metagenomic analysis of these 'phylogenetic frontiers' would then be employed to discover natural products from biosynthetic pathways that diverged deep in evolutionary time, maximizing the chance of novel scaffold discovery.

ideas.title: Assessing collateral damage to a mutualistic partner using a host-parasite model
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that an antifungal compound targeting a fungal parasite would not affect the host's mutualistic fungus. The analogy comes from Human Microbiome & Antibiotic Therapy, where solutions involve assessing collateral damage to beneficial bacteria during treatment of a pathogen. The adapted idea is to treat the termite-fungus mutualism as an integrated system, similar to a host-microbiome system. Instead of simple isolation assays, develop a co-culture or microcosm model to test macrotermycins, using functional metrics (like metabolite exchange or fungal garden health) to quantify harm to the mutualistic partner, thereby revealing potential risks of disrupting the symbiosis.

ideas.title: Integrated multi-layer de-repression system for robust activation of silent BGCs in Amycolatopsis sp. M39
ideas.description: This redesigned approach addresses the multi-layered silencing of BGCs by deploying a coordinated, inducible genetic toolkit. First, it uses comparative transcriptomics of related Amycolatopsis strains to identify potential pathway-specific activators and integrates their genes under an inducible promoter. Concurrently, a CRISPRa system targets both the BGC promoter and local transcriptional repressors. Finally, key biosynthetic genes are placed under the control of a strong, tunable synthetic promoter to ensure high-level expression once repression is lifted, thereby guaranteeing robust production of the encoded secondary metabolites.

ideas.title: Ecology-Informed Activation of Silent Biosynthetic Gene Clusters Across Kingdoms
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond simply sourcing organisms from non-plant kingdoms to directly solving the central problem of silent biosynthetic potential. It begins with meta-omics profiling of complex microbial communities from underexplored biomes to map both the presence and in situ expression of BGCs. The most promising silent BGCs are then targeted for activation by cultivating their host organisms in microfluidic chips that simulate key environmental parameters and by introducing specific genetic or ecological triggers identified from the omics data. This creates a targeted, hypothesis-driven discovery pipeline that maximizes the yield of novel chemistry from any source by forcing expressed production of the encoded metabolites.

ideas.title: Sequence-first, function-agnostic pipeline for natural product discovery from inaccessible biosynthetic reservoirs
ideas.description: This approach inverts the traditional pipeline by starting with large-scale environmental sequencing to map biosynthetic gene cluster (BGC) diversity from complex, uncultivable microbiomes and extreme environments. Candidate BGCs are prioritized computationally for novelty and expressed in engineered heterologous hosts, bypassing cultivation and traditional extraction bottlenecks. The resulting expressed compound libraries are then fed into integrated structure elucidation and high-throughput phenotypic and target-based bioassays. This system fundamentally de-risks discovery by guaranteeing access to genetically encoded novelty before any resource-intensive chemical or biological screening is performed.

ideas.title: Apply astronomical spectral pattern matching for microbial metabolite analog discovery
ideas.description: This idea adapts the astronomical method of spectral template matching for rapid object identification to the problem of finding natural product analogs. In astronomy, unknown celestial objects are classified by matching their observed spectra against a library of known spectral templates. The adapted solution involves creating a curated library of MS/MS spectral templates for key natural product scaffolds, such as macrotermycin. When analyzing LC-MS/MS data from new bacterial strains, their fragmentation spectra are computationally screened against this template library using high-speed spectral similarity algorithms. This direct pattern-matching approach allows for the immediate prioritization of strains producing known analog families and quickly highlights spectral clusters corresponding to potential novel analogs adjacent to the matched core scaffolds, streamlining the discovery pipeline compared to building complete molecular networks for every dataset.

ideas.title: Functional validation of host-restricted niche through reciprocal transplantation and fitness assays
ideas.description: This redesigned study first conducts a phylogenetically-guided, deep metagenomic survey of the actinomycete across a rigorously defined set of potential alternative environments, including other termite species, related insect hosts, and relevant free-living habitats. The core innovation is the subsequent experimental phase: cultured isolates or genetically barcoded strains are reciprocally transplanted into sterile microcosms mimicking these alternative environments, and their survival, growth, and compound production are quantitatively measured against host-termite conditions. This approach moves beyond detection to test the fundamental ecological requirement of the niche—the ability to maintain a population—providing mechanistic, causal evidence for whether the host termite is an obligate niche.

ideas.title: Mechanism-Deconvoluted SAR of Macrotermycins via Integrated Target and Cellular Profiling
ideas.description: This project systematically synthesizes a focused library of macrotermycin analogs designed to probe specific regions of the molecule. Each analog is simultaneously profiled in three parallel assays: 1) a biophysical target engagement assay, 2) a standard whole-cell MIC determination, and 3) assays against engineered strains overexpressing major efflux pumps or degradation enzymes. This integrated dataset directly maps structural modifications to their specific functional consequences—distinguishing between analogs that lose target binding, those that retain binding but fail to penetrate cells or are effluxed, and those that overcome these barriers. The resulting SAR is therefore mechanism-aware, guiding the design of next-generation analogs with optimized target potency and evasion of innate microbial defenses.
